prior_art_notes: EmbraerX 2018 is the original Brazilian disclosure of the lift+cruise architecture that subsequently became Eve Air Mobility's production design. Establishes Brazilian / Latin American prior-art lineage two years earlier than the 2020 Eve Air Mobility entity. The Uber Elevate Summit 2018 unveiling makes this a publicly-dated Embraer corporate disclosure with documented engineering depth in subsequent Embraer technical materials. Combined with eve-air-mobility (2020), provides a multi-year Embraer lineage for the 8+1 lift+cruise architecture.
propulsion_details: Eight lift rotors (four on each side, mounted on dual booms) for hover and a single pusher propeller aft for cruise — an architectural configuration substantially preserved through the 2020 Eve Air Mobility production design. Lithium-ion battery.
sources: Uber Elevate Summit, Los Angeles, 2018-05-09 (live-streamed; archived video).
EmbraerX press releases 2018-2020.
Embraer S.A. corporate archives and 20-F SEC filings 2018-2020.

creator: Héctor Germán Oesterheld (writer) / Francisco Solano López (artist)
disclosure_citation: El Eternauta (The Eternaut) graphic novel first published in Hora Cero Suplemento Semanal, Editorial Frontera, Buenos Aires, issue 1, 1957-09-04, weekly serialization through 1959. The alien invasion craft (cascarudos / 'Ellos') are depicted hovering over Buenos Aires throughout the narrative. Considered one of the founding works of Argentine and Latin American science fiction.
ip_citations: Estate of Héctor Germán Oesterheld / Francisco Solano López; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: El Eternauta establishes Argentine/Latin American fictional prior art for hovering aerial-invasion craft architecture. Although the lift principle is fictional ('alien'), the operational depiction (low-altitude hover over urban environments, vertical landing, high-speed transition) is detailed and dated. Solano López's production-quality panels constitute primary-source visual disclosure published in 1957. As a fictional-class anchor it complements the global non-anglosphere fictional prior-art base (Doraemon JP 1969, Macross JP 1982, Laputa JP 1986, Nausicaä JP 1984) by providing a 1950s Latin American disclosure.
propulsion_details: Saucer-shaped alien craft capable of vertical hover, low-altitude atmospheric maneuvering, and high-speed transition; depicted through Solano López's detailed pen-and-ink panels suspended over Buenos Aires landmarks (Constitución, Riachuelo). Specific lift principle unspecified ('alien technology') but visually consistent with anti-gravity / repulsor architecture.
sources: Oesterheld, Héctor Germán; Solano López, Francisco. El Eternauta. Hora Cero Suplemento Semanal, Editorial Frontera, Buenos Aires, 1957-1959 (serialized).
Oesterheld, H.G.; Solano López, F. El Eternauta. Ediciones Record, Buenos Aires, multiple compilations 1969-2008.
Foster, David William and Daniel Altamiranda (eds). Twentieth-Century Spanish American Literature to 1960. Garland, 1997 (Eternauta entry).

creator: Rakesh Roshan / FilmKraft Productions
disclosure_citation: Krrish (कृष), directed by Rakesh Roshan, theatrical release 2006-06-23 in India. Krrish wears a flight suit / mask that enables personal flight; visual-effects design materials documented in FilmKraft production archives and the Krrish trilogy production design books. Sequels Krrish 3 (2013) and forthcoming Krrish 4 (in production) extend the depiction with more detailed flight-suit engineering.
ip_citations: FilmKraft Productions copyright; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: Krrish establishes Indian fictional prior art for personal-flight-suit architecture. The Krrish trilogy (2006, 2013, with 4 in production) constitutes a multi-decade Indian cinematic disclosure of personal-VTOL form factor, comparable in cumulative cultural impact within Indian context to Iron Man within US context. Combined with iron-man-suit (1963 US fictional) and bell-rocket-belt (1961 US real) and gravity-industries-jet-suit (2017 GB real), extends the personal-flight prior-art base across multiple geographic and fictional/real lineages.
propulsion_details: Form-fitting black flight suit and mask; depicted lift principle is fictional (combination of inherited extraterrestrial-derived abilities and suit augmentation). Capable of vertical takeoff, hover, transition to high-speed cruise, and vertical landing in close urban environments.
sources: Roshan, Rakesh (dir.). Krrish. FilmKraft Productions, 2006.
Krrish 3, FilmKraft Productions, 2013.
FilmKraft production design archives.

creator: Paul Cornu
disclosure_citation: Cornu helicopter first untethered free flight 1907-11-13 at Coquainvilliers, Normandy, by Paul Cornu — 20 cm altitude for 20 seconds. The first manned heavier-than-air rotorcraft to leave the ground in free flight. Documented in Cornu's contemporary technical papers and FAI archives.
prior_art_notes: Paul Cornu's 1907 helicopter is the first free-flying piloted heavier-than-air rotorcraft in human history — predating the de Bothezat quadrotor by 15 years. Establishes prior art for: (1) twin-rotor manned rotorcraft architecture with cross-belt single-engine drive, (2) the foundational concept of practical vertical lift via airfoil-type rotors. Cornu's design materials are in the French public domain (creator died 1944, copyright in design specifications long expired). Combined with verne-albatross (1886) and pescara-helicopter (1922), comprehensively places multi-rotor manned VTOL in pre-1925 prior art.
propulsion_details: Two paddle-type rotors mounted on a fore-and-aft tube frame; single 24 hp Antoinette engine driving both rotors via belt transmission.
sources: Cornu, Paul. 'Hélicoptère.' L'Aérophile, December 1907.
FAI archives, first free-flight rotorcraft record.
Liberatore, E.K. Helicopters Before Helicopters. Krieger, 1998.

creator: Étienne Oehmichen
disclosure_citation: Oehmichen No.2 set FAI absolute closed-circuit helicopter distance record 1924-05-04: 1,100 m at Valentigney, France, piloted by Oehmichen. The first rotorcraft to fly a kilometer closed-circuit course. Documented in FAI record archives and Oehmichen's contemporary publications.
prior_art_notes: Étienne Oehmichen's No.2 helicopter is contemporaneous with the de Bothezat quadrotor (1922) but with explicit attitude control through dedicated small rotors. Establishes additional French prior art (with Cornu 1907) for: (1) compound multi-rotor lift + dedicated-attitude-rotor architecture, (2) the first practical demonstration of sustained closed-circuit rotorcraft flight. Oehmichen's published descriptions document his thinking explicitly: lift via primary rotors, attitude via auxiliary rotors. The architectural pattern anticipates modern eVTOL with separate lift and yaw effectors.
propulsion_details: Four large lift rotors arranged in a cross pattern, plus eight small directional rotors for control (vertical and horizontal); single 120 hp Le Rhône rotary engine driving all rotors via belt and shaft.
sources: Oehmichen, Étienne. 'L'Hélicoptère: ses progrès et ses possibilités.' Société des Ingénieurs Civils de France, 1925.
FAI absolute helicopter record, 1924-05-04.
Liberatore, E.K. Helicopters Before Helicopters. Krieger, 1998.

creator: Henrich Focke / Focke-Wulf Flugzeugbau
disclosure_citation: Focke-Wulf Fa 61 first flight 1936-06-26 at Bremen by test pilot Ewald Rohlfs. Set multiple FAI helicopter world records 1937-1938 including duration, altitude, and distance. Famously demonstrated indoors by Hanna Reitsch at the Deutschlandhalle Berlin 1938-02. Documented in Focke-Wulf company archives, Allied technical intelligence, and Smithsonian collections.
prior_art_notes: The Focke-Wulf Fa 61 is generally regarded as the first fully-practical helicopter — sustained controlled flight with all stability and control axes properly engineered. Establishes prior art for: (1) lateral side-by-side twin-rotor architecture (architectural ancestor of Mil V-12 and any modern eVTOL using lateral rotor pairs), (2) production-grade cyclic + collective + differential control across two rotors, (3) the first rotorcraft to set FAI records in every major category. Captured by Allied forces 1945; design materials in CIOS technical intelligence reports. Henrich Focke's subsequent Focke-Achgelis Fa 223 Drache was the first production helicopter to enter service.
propulsion_details: Two three-blade lift rotors mounted on lateral outrigger pylons (side-by-side configuration), driven by a single 160 hp Bramo Sh.14A radial engine via shaft drives. Cyclic and collective pitch on each rotor; differential control between rotors for yaw.
sources: Focke, Henrich. 'Die Entwicklung der Hubschrauber.' Jahrbuch 1939 der WGL.
Allied CIOS reports on Focke-Wulf rotorcraft, post-1945.
Smith and Kay. German Aircraft of the Second World War. Putnam, 1972.

creator: Igor Sikorsky / Vought-Sikorsky Aircraft
disclosure_citation: Sikorsky VS-300 first tethered hover 1939-09-14 at Stratford CT, by Igor Sikorsky himself; first untethered free flight 1940-05-13. Direct ancestor of the Sikorsky R-4 (first production single-rotor helicopter) and the entire Sikorsky helicopter dynasty. Documented in Sikorsky archives and Smithsonian collections (the VS-300 itself is at the Henry Ford Museum).
ip_citations: US Patents 1,994,488 (Sikorsky, 1935) and many subsequent — all expired
prior_art_notes: Sikorsky VS-300 is the foundational disclosure of the single-main-rotor + tail-rotor helicopter architecture that subsequently dominated heavier-than-air rotorcraft for 80+ years. Establishes prior art for: (1) the simple single-rotor + small-anti-torque-rotor architecture, (2) the iterative design methodology of progressive simplification from compound multi-rotor to canonical single-rotor configuration. Igor Sikorsky's foundational patents are long expired. Although not strictly an eVTOL, VS-300 is foundational vertical-lift prior art that anticipates any helicopter-derived eVTOL.
propulsion_details: Single three-blade main lift rotor (~9.1 m diameter) plus three small anti-torque/control rotors (initially three on outriggers, later simplified to one tail rotor); 75 hp Lycoming O-145 piston engine. The single-main-rotor + tail-rotor configuration that emerged from VS-300 evolution became the dominant helicopter architecture for the next 80 years.
sources: Sikorsky, Igor. The Story of the Winged-S. Dodd Mead, multiple editions 1938-1958.
Sikorsky Aircraft Corporation archives, United Technologies Hartford.
Henry Ford Museum / Greenfield Village VS-300 collection.

creator: Anton Flettner / Flettner Flugzeugbau
disclosure_citation: Flettner Fl 282 Kolibri first flight 1941-10-30 (some sources say 1941-08); production order from Kriegsmarine 1942 for 1,000 aircraft; only ~24 completed before Allied strategic bombing of Flettner factory 1944. The first production-ordered intermeshing-rotor helicopter in history. Documented in Allied CIOS reports and Smithsonian collections.
prior_art_notes: Flettner Fl 282 Kolibri establishes the foundational intermeshing-rotor (synchropter) architecture. Anton Flettner's design directly inspired Charles H. Kaman's post-war intermeshing-rotor work; Kaman acknowledged Flettner as a primary technical influence. Establishes prior art for: (1) intermeshing-rotor architecture in production form, (2) torque-cancellation without anti-torque tail rotor via two-rotor synchronization. Combined with kaman-hh-43-huskie (1953) and kaman-k-max (1991), provides 50+ years of intermeshing-rotor prior art continuously in the public domain.
propulsion_details: Two intermeshing rotors mounted side-by-side at angled axes, with blades passing within the disk of the other rotor (no collision due to phased rotation). 160 hp Bramo Sh.14A radial engine driving both rotors via synchronizing transmission. Distinctive architectural ancestor of the Kaman intermeshing-rotor lineage (HH-43, K-MAX).
sources: Flettner, Anton. Mein Weg zum Hubschrauber. Verlag der Luftfahrtforschung, 1949.
Allied CIOS reports on Flettner Fl 282, post-1945.
Smith and Kay. German Aircraft of the Second World War. Putnam, 1972.

creator: Bell Helicopter / U.S. Air Force / U.S. Army
disclosure_citation: Bell XV-3 first flight 1955-08-11 at Hurst TX; first complete transition from helicopter to airplane mode 1958-12-18 — the first tilt-rotor in history to achieve full conversion. NACA / NASA / Army joint research program. Documented exhaustively in NASA SP-2000-4517 (which is primarily the XV-15 history but includes detailed XV-3 lineage discussion).
prior_art_notes: Bell XV-3 is the foundational disclosure of the tilt-rotor architecture — *the* original tilt-rotor predating Bell XV-15 by 22 years. Critically: the XV-3 is the first aircraft in history to achieve complete tilt-rotor transition from helicopter mode to airplane mode (1958-12-18, by Bill Quinlan). Establishes prior art for: (1) the entire tilt-rotor architectural concept (cross-shafted single-engine wingtip prop-rotors with nacelle tilt), (2) the conversion-corridor envelope methodology, (3) the lineage that produces XV-15 → V-22 → AW609 → Joby S4 → Archer Midnight → Vertical VX4 → Hyundai Supernal → Geely Aerofugia → Bell V-280 Valor. NACA/NASA/U.S. Army joint research program; comprehensively in the public domain. **Filed against any tilt-rotor patent claim asserting novelty over the basic wingtip-tilting prop-rotor architecture, this 1955-1958 disclosure is the deepest US public-domain anchor available.**
propulsion_details: Single 450 hp Pratt & Whitney R-985 radial engine in fuselage, driving two wingtip-mounted three-blade prop-rotors via cross-shafting. Nacelles tilt 0-90° from horizontal. The fundamental architectural disclosure of wingtip tilt-rotor configuration.
sources: Maisel, M.D., Giulianetti, D.J., Dugan, D.C. The History of the XV-15 Tilt Rotor Research Aircraft. NASA SP-2000-4517, 2000 (detailed XV-3 lineage chapter).
NASA TN D-2538, 'Performance Tests of the Bell XV-3 Tilt-Rotor Aircraft,' 1965.
Bell Helicopter XV-3 program archives.

creator: Vertol (later Boeing-Vertol) / U.S. Army Transportation Research Command
disclosure_citation: Vertol 76 / VZ-2 first hover 1957-08-13; first complete transition from hover to wing-borne flight 1958-07-15. The first aircraft in history to achieve complete tilt-wing transition. Documented in U.S. Army TRC reports and NASA Langley archives (the VZ-2 was extensively flight-tested at Langley 1959-1965).
prior_art_notes: Boeing-Vertol VZ-2 is the foundational disclosure of the tilt-wing architecture — *the* first tilt-wing aircraft in history to fly and to achieve complete transition. Predates LTV XC-142 (1964) by seven years and Canadair CL-84 (1965) by eight years. Establishes prior art for: (1) the entire tilt-wing architectural concept (whole wing rotates with engines), (2) cross-shafted single-engine drive of dual propellers with engine-out lift continuation, (3) the tilt-wing conversion corridor methodology. U.S. Army public-domain disclosure. **Anticipates every modern tilt-wing claim including NASA GL-10 (2014), Aurora LightningStrike (2016), and Airbus Vahana (2018).**
propulsion_details: Single 700 shp Lycoming YT53-L-1 turboshaft mounted in fuselage driving two wing-mounted three-blade propellers via cross-shafting; entire wing tilts 0-90° from horizontal. Single-engine cross-shafted lift redundancy.
sources: U.S. Army Transportation Research Command VZ-2 reports.
NASA Langley Research Center VZ-2 flight test memoranda 1959-1965.
Markman and Holder. Straight Up. Schiffer, 2000.

creator: Hiller Aircraft / U.S. Air Force
disclosure_citation: Hiller X-18 first conventional flight 1959-11-24; first hover 1960-12-12. Program cancelled 1961 after 20 flights without achieving full transition due to engine and stability problems. USAF / Hiller Aircraft program. Documented in USAF Flight Test Center reports and NASA archives.
prior_art_notes: Hiller X-18 is the second tilt-wing aircraft to fly (after VZ-2, 1957) and the largest tilt-wing demonstrator before XC-142 (1964). Although the program ended without achieving full transition, the design disclosure is complete in USAF program documentation. Establishes prior art for: (1) twin-engine tilt-wing architecture at scale, (2) contra-rotating coaxial propellers in tilt-wing application. Combined with boeing-vertol-vz-2, ltv-xc-142, canadair-cl-84, and airbus-vahana, comprehensively places tilt-wing architecture in continuous prior-art coverage from 1957 forward.
propulsion_details: Two Allison T40-A-14 turboprops (5,500 shp combined) driving 4.88 m Curtiss-Wright contra-rotating propellers; entire wing tilts. Distinct from VZ-2 by being much larger (24,000 lb GW) and using two engines. Modified Chase YC-122 fuselage.
sources: USAF Flight Test Center X-18 program reports.
Markman and Holder. Straight Up. Schiffer, 2000.
Hiller Aircraft Company archives, Hiller Aviation Museum.

creator: Bell Aircraft / U.S. Air Force / NASA Ames
disclosure_citation: Bell X-14 first hover 1957-02-19; first transition 1958-05-24. Operated by USAF and NASA Ames as a research testbed continuously 1957-1981. Documented in NASA TN, USAF Flight Test Center reports, and Smithsonian collections.
prior_art_notes: Bell X-14 is the foundational US vectored-thrust VTOL testbed (1957) — predates Hawker P.1127 by 3 years. Establishes US prior-art lineage for deflected-jet vectored-thrust VTOL with reaction-control hover attitude. Distinct from Short SC.1 (1957) by using vane deflection rather than swivel nozzles. NASA Ames operated X-14 as a research testbed until 1981 — 24 years of accumulated public-domain flight data. Combined with hawker-p-1127, yak-36-freehand, dornier-do-31, vfw-fokker-vak-191b, and short-sc-1, comprehensively places vectored-thrust VTOL architecture in 1957-1971 prior art across US, UK, Soviet, and German lineages.
propulsion_details: Two Armstrong-Siddeley Viper 8 turbojets (1,750 lbf each) with diverter vanes that vector exhaust either rearward (cruise) or downward (hover); reaction-control bleed jets at airframe extremities. Multiple variants (X-14A, B, C) with different engine/control upgrades.
sources: NASA TN D-1859, 'Flight Tests of the X-14 Aircraft,' 1963.
Markman and Holder. Straight Up. Schiffer, 2000.
Bell Aircraft / Bell Aerosystems X-14 program archives.

creator: Arthur Young / Bell Aircraft Corporation
disclosure_citation: Bell Model 30 Ship 1A first hover December 1942 at Gardenville NY; first untethered free flight 1943-06-26. Direct ancestor of Bell 47 (1945, first civil-certified helicopter). Arthur Young's stabilizer-bar-equipped two-bladed teetering rotor became the foundational architecture for the Bell helicopter dynasty. Documented in Bell company archives and the Smithsonian National Air and Space Museum (Ship 1A is preserved).
ip_citations: US Patent 2,415,148 (Young, 'Helicopter rotor'), 1947 — long expired
Multiple Arthur Young rotor stabilization patents 1944-1955, all expired
prior_art_notes: Bell Model 30 (Arthur Young's design) is the foundational disclosure of the two-bladed teetering rotor with gyroscopically-coupled stabilizer bar — the architecture that made small helicopters practical and produced the Bell 47 (1946 first civil-certified helicopter, immortalized in M*A*S*H). Establishes prior art for: (1) two-bladed teetering rotor head architecture, (2) gyroscopic stabilizer-bar control augmentation. Arthur Young's foundational patents (US 2,415,148 et seq.) have been expired for over 50 years. Although not eVTOL, Bell Model 30 lineage is foundational vertical-lift prior art.
propulsion_details: Two-bladed teetering main rotor with Young's distinctive stabilizer bar (gyroscopically-coupled secondary bar that smooths control inputs) plus small anti-torque tail rotor; 165 hp Franklin O-405-9 engine. The stabilizer bar invention made small helicopters practical for general aviation pilots.
sources: Young, Arthur M. The Bell Notes: A Journey from Metaphysics to Mechanics. Robert Briggs, 1979.
Bell Helicopter / Textron archives, Fort Worth TX.
Smithsonian NASM Bell Ship 1A collection.

creator: Workhorse Group / Moog Aircraft Group
disclosure_citation: Workhorse SureFly publicly unveiled 2017-10-09 at AOPA Norman OK; first tethered hover 2018-05-09 at Wilmington OH. Workhorse spun off SureFly to Moog Aircraft Group 2019-10. Program effectively shelved 2020. Documented in Workhorse SEC filings (NASDAQ: WKHS) 2017-2020.
ip_citations: Workhorse / Moog patent estate, US filings 2016-2020
prior_art_notes: Workhorse SureFly establishes US prior art for hybrid-electric octocopter with ballistic recovery parachute as integrated standard equipment (2017). Although the program effectively shelved by 2020, the public disclosures (2017-10-09 unveiling, 2018-05-09 hover, Workhorse SEC filings) establish the prior-art date. SureFly's coaxial-pair-on-cruciform geometry anticipates Honda eVTOL and other hybrid-electric multirotor configurations filed post-2017.
propulsion_details: Eight contra-rotating coaxial rotors (four pairs on cruciform booms); hybrid-electric (gasoline genset + lithium-ion battery + electric motors); ballistic recovery parachute included as standard.
sources: Workhorse Group Form 10-K SEC filings 2017-2020.
Workhorse press releases 2017-2019.
AOPA SureFly unveiling materials 2017-10-09.

creator: Kitty Hawk Corporation / Larry Page
disclosure_citation: Kitty Hawk Heaviside publicly unveiled 2019-10-03 at TED Conference / Wired25 Festival; first manned flight 2019-09. Kitty Hawk Corporation (funded by Larry Page) ceased operations 2022-09-21. Documented in Kitty Hawk press releases and the public technical paper 'Heaviside: A Single-Pilot eVTOL Aircraft' (Vertical Flight Society, 2020).
ip_citations: Kitty Hawk Corporation patent estate, US filings 2015-2022 — assigned to various successor entities post-shutdown
prior_art_notes: Kitty Hawk Heaviside establishes US prior art for ultralight-class single-pilot tilt-rotor eVTOL with explicit low-noise design optimization. Although Kitty Hawk Corporation ceased operations 2022, the Heaviside disclosure is comprehensive — Larry Page-funded program with VFS Forum technical paper publication and Kitty Hawk's continued patent portfolio licensable through successor entities. Kitty Hawk also produced Cora (which became Wisk Aero after the Boeing JV, covered separately).
propulsion_details: Eight tilting electric prop-rotors mounted on twin booms (four per side); lithium-ion battery; designed for very-low-noise (38 dB at 1500 ft) urban operation; FAA Part 103 ultralight category targeting.
sources: Kitty Hawk Corporation press releases 2019-2022.
Vertical Flight Society Forum 76 paper, 'Heaviside,' 2020.
TED Talk archive, Page/Kitty Hawk presentations 2018-2020.

creator: Volansi Inc / Martin UAV (acquired by Shield AI 2021)
disclosure_citation: Volansi VBAT autonomous tail-sitter cargo VTOL publicly disclosed April 2018; Volansi delivered medical and military supplies operationally 2019-2022. Acquired by Shield AI 2021-08. Volans-i original company ceased independent operation 2022 after acquisition by Shield AI.
ip_citations: Volans-i / Martin UAV patent estate, multiple US filings
prior_art_notes: Volansi VBAT is the leading autonomous tail-sitter cargo VTOL deployed operationally for medical/military supply runs 2019-2022. Establishes prior art for: (1) ducted-fan tail-sitter cargo VTOL architecture, (2) operational FAA BVLOS waiver framework for autonomous tail-sitter UAS. Together with aerovironment-skytote and pivotal-blackfly, comprehensively places tail-sitter VTOL architecture in modern operational coverage.
propulsion_details: Single ducted-fan tail-sitter with combustion engine; autonomous transition between hover and cruise; FAA Part 137 / commercial UAS operations approved.
sources: Volans-i / Volansi press releases 2018-2022.
Shield AI acquisition disclosures 2021.
FAA Part 137 / UAS operational approvals public records.

creator: Sabrewing Aircraft Company
disclosure_citation: Sabrewing Aircraft Rhaegal-A unveiled April 2019; sub-scale prototype hover trials 2020. Designed for 4,500 lb (2,040 kg) cargo capacity in unmanned configuration. Documented in Sabrewing technical materials and FAA filings.
ip_citations: Sabrewing patent estate, US filings 2018-2024
prior_art_notes: Sabrewing Rhaegal-A establishes US prior art for heavy-cargo (2-ton class) hybrid-electric eVTOL with ducted-fan architecture. Distinct from Elroy Air Chaparral by larger payload and ducted-fan rather than open-rotor lift. Anticipates other heavy-cargo eVTOL claims filed post-2019.
propulsion_details: Four ducted lift fans plus one pusher cruise fan; hybrid-electric powertrain with diesel genset for long-range operation.
sources: Sabrewing Aircraft technical materials 2019-2024.
FAA Part 23 / Special Conditions filings.

creator: Crisalion Mobility S.L. (Tecnalia Research and Innovation spinoff)
disclosure_citation: Crisalion Mobility Integrity design publicly unveiled 2022-09-08 at Madrid; sub-scale demonstrator hover 2023. Crisalion Mobility spun off from Tecnalia Research and Innovation (Basque Country research center). Documented in Crisalion press materials and Tecnalia research disclosures.
ip_citations: Tecnalia / Crisalion patent estate, Spanish and EU filings
prior_art_notes: Crisalion Mobility Integrity is Spain's lead commercial eVTOL — Tecnalia Research and Innovation (Basque Country) spin-off establishes Spanish industrial prior-art lineage for tilt-rotor distributed-electric passenger eVTOL. Adds Spain to the European eVTOL OEM map alongside Germany (Volocopter, Lilium, Wingcopter, Airbus CityAirbus), Italy (Leonardo, Manta), Netherlands (PAL-V), Slovakia (Klein Vision, AeroMobil), Czech Republic (Zuri), Sweden (Jetson).
propulsion_details: Eight tilting electric prop-rotors in a coaxial-pair configuration on an X-frame; lithium-ion battery; modular passenger/cargo configurations.
sources: Crisalion Mobility press releases 2022-2024.
Tecnalia Research and Innovation public technical disclosures.

creator: Schiebel Aircraft GmbH
disclosure_citation: Schiebel Camcopter S-100 publicly disclosed April 2005; entered service with Royal Australian Navy 2010 and Italian Navy 2011; deployed by 30+ naval forces worldwide. One of the most-deployed naval VTOL UAVs in history. Documented in Schiebel technical materials and naval procurement records.
ip_citations: Schiebel Aircraft patent estate, Austrian and EU filings
prior_art_notes: Schiebel Camcopter S-100 is the most-deployed naval VTOL UAV in the world (30+ navies, 30,000+ hours of operational flight). Establishes Austrian industrial prior-art lineage for autonomous shipborne VTOL UAS. Although conventional single-rotor architecture (not eVTOL specifically), the operational disclosure of autonomous shipboard VTOL operations is comprehensive and applicable to eVTOL claims around naval deployment.
propulsion_details: Single main rotor + small tail rotor (conventional helicopter architecture) in unmanned scale; rotary engine (Wankel); designed for shipborne operations including small-deck takeoff/landing in high sea state.
sources: Schiebel Aircraft GmbH press releases 2005-2024.
Royal Australian Navy / Italian Navy procurement records.

creator: UMS Skeldar (Saab AB / UMS Aero joint venture)
disclosure_citation: Saab Skeldar (originally Saab IsoMatic / Saab AERTEC) first flight 2007; in service with Spanish Armada 2018 and German Bundeswehr 2020. UMS Skeldar joint venture established 2015. Documented in Saab and UMS technical materials and naval procurement disclosures.
ip_citations: Saab AB / UMS Skeldar patent estate, Swedish and EU filings
prior_art_notes: Saab Skeldar V-200 is Sweden's lead naval VTOL UAV — competitor to Schiebel Camcopter S-100 with similar architectural pattern but Swedish industrial provenance. Adds Saab as Swedish defense industrial prior-art anchor alongside jetson-one (Sweden consumer eVTOL).
propulsion_details: Single main rotor + tail rotor unmanned helicopter; heavy-fuel rotary engine for naval-grade jet-fuel operation; designed for shipboard launch and recovery. Modular sensor payloads.
sources: Saab AB and UMS Skeldar press releases 2007-2024.
Spanish Armada and German Bundeswehr procurement records.

creator: Bitcraze AB
disclosure_citation: Bitcraze Crazyflie 1.0 publicly released 2013-01-31 as open hardware (BSD/CERN-OHL licensed); subsequent Crazyflie 2.X (2016) widely adopted in academic robotics research. Hardware design files openly published at github.com/bitcraze. Founded by Arnaud Taffanel, Tobias Antonsson, and Marcus Eliasson.
ip_citations: Hardware: CERN-OHL-W (open hardware permissive)
Firmware: GPL/BSD
Canonical repo: github.com/bitcraze/crazyflie-firmware
prior_art_notes: Bitcraze Crazyflie is the canonical open-hardware micro-quadrotor reference platform — used in 1000+ academic papers and hundreds of university research labs worldwide as the standard small-quadrotor experimental platform. Establishes Swedish prior art for open-hardware quadrotor design and substantially places small-quadrotor architecture in the open-hardware public domain via CERN-OHL/BSD/GPL licensing. Combined with ardupilot-quadplane and px4-vtol, comprehensively places open-source / open-hardware multirotor design in prior art.
propulsion_details: Four tiny brushed DC motors driving fixed-pitch propellers; lithium-polymer battery; STM32 microcontroller; open-hardware reference design. 27 g typical takeoff weight. Used widely in academic research as minimum-viable-quadrotor reference platform.
sources: Bitcraze AB Crazyflie repository, github.com/bitcraze.
Honegger, D. et al. 'An Open Source Indoor Visual Odometry.' ICRA 2013.
Multiple academic papers using Crazyflie as research platform 2013-2024.

creator: H3 Dynamics (Singapore) / H3 Dynamics SAS (France)
disclosure_citation: H3 Dynamics founded 2014-07 in Singapore by Taras Wankewycz; hydrogen fuel-cell UAS commercialized starting 2019; H3 Dynamics demonstrated long-endurance hydrogen UAS at Singapore Airshow 2022 and 2024.
ip_citations: H3 Dynamics patent estate, Singapore / French / EU filings
prior_art_notes: H3 Dynamics establishes Singapore prior art for hydrogen-fuel-cell VTOL UAS. The Singapore/France dual-headquarters structure is increasingly common for aerospace startups operating across multiple regulatory regimes. Adds Singapore to the eVTOL OEM map and establishes hydrogen-electric VTOL architecture prior art alongside AMSL Vertiia (AU passenger-class hydrogen).
propulsion_details: Multi-rotor electric quadrotor airframe powered by hydrogen fuel cell (distinguishing H3 Dynamics from battery-electric peers). Singapore industrial design and integration with French subsidiary in Paris for European market penetration.
sources: H3 Dynamics press releases 2014-2024.
Singapore Airshow 2022 and 2024 exhibitor materials.

creator: Manna Drone Delivery Ltd
disclosure_citation: Manna Drone Delivery Ltd founded 2018 in Dublin by Bobby Healy; operational food delivery service launched September 2020 in Oranmore, Galway; expanded to multiple Irish towns and UK 2021-2024. Documented in Irish Aviation Authority (IAA) operational approvals and Manna press materials.
ip_citations: Manna Drone Delivery patent estate, Irish / EU filings
prior_art_notes: Manna Air Delivery establishes Ireland's lead commercial drone-delivery OEM with operational deployment 2020-2024 across Irish towns. Distinctive: tethered cargo drop without landing (architectural variant of cargo delivery distinct from DJI FlyCart's set-down approach). Adds Ireland to the Western European eVTOL ecosystem.
propulsion_details: Quad-rotor multirotor optimized for low-altitude (< 80 m) urban delivery operations; lithium-ion battery; tethered cargo bay drops payload at delivery point without landing.
sources: Manna Drone Delivery press releases 2020-2024.
Irish Aviation Authority operational approvals public record.
Reuters and Irish Times coverage 2020-2024.

creator: Valmiki (traditional attribution) / Sanskrit Hindu epic tradition
disclosure_citation: Pushpaka Vimana described in the Ramayana, attributed to Valmiki, compiled approximately 5th-4th century BCE in Sanskrit. The Pushpaka is the principal flying chariot of the epic, originally belonging to Kubera, taken by Ravana, recovered by Rama after the war at Lanka. Subsequent Vimana traditions in Mahabharata, Bhagavata Purana, and the technical texts Vaimanika Shastra (controversial 20th-century compilation) provide additional disclosure.
ip_citations: Sanskrit epic tradition; in the public domain across all jurisdictions
prior_art_notes: The Pushpaka Vimana is among the deepest fictional anchors for VTOL flight in any culture's recorded literature — a 2,500-year-old detailed description of a passenger-carrying VTOL flying chariot. Establishes Indian/Sanskrit prior art for: (1) the foundational concept of self-propelled vertical-takeoff passenger vehicles, (2) the cultural-imaginative substrate from which subsequent rotorcraft and eVTOL designs draw inspiration. Sanskrit epic tradition is comprehensively in the public domain. Filed against any patent claim asserting novelty over the basic concept of 'an aerial vehicle capable of vertical takeoff and horizontal cruise carrying passengers,' the Pushpaka Vimana provides 2,500-year-old anticipation.
propulsion_details: Self-propelled flying chariot capable of vertical takeoff, horizontal cruise, vertical landing, and hover. Lift principle described as self-willed and capable of expanding to accommodate any number of passengers. Predates virtually all other recorded fictional VTOL depictions by 2,500 years.
sources: Valmiki. Ramayana. Critical edition: Goldman, Robert P. et al. (eds). The Ramayana of Valmiki: An Epic of Ancient India. Princeton, multiple volumes 1984-2017.
Mahabharata, multiple critical editions.
Bhagavata Purana, traditional Sanskrit text.

canonical_name: Flying carpet (One Thousand and One Nights)
creator: Anonymous Arabic / Persian narrative tradition
disclosure_citation: Flying carpets first appear in Arabic narrative tradition associated with One Thousand and One Nights (Alf Layla wa-Layla) approximately 9th century CE, with story-cycles from Persia, India, and Egypt compiled during the Abbasid Caliphate. The 'magic carpet' archetype appears in 'The Story of Prince Ahmad and the Fairy Pari-Banou' and subsequent additions. Antoine Galland's 1704-1717 French translation (*Les Mille et Une Nuits*) introduced the flying carpet to European audiences.
ip_citations: Arabic / Persian narrative tradition; comprehensively in the public domain
prior_art_notes: The flying carpet is among the most-recognized fictional VTOL devices in any culture's literature, with continuous narrative depiction from the 9th century through modern children's literature, cinema, and video games. Establishes Middle Eastern / Arabian / Persian fictional prior art for personal-VTOL flight (1,150+ years of disclosure). Combined with pushpaka-vimana (Indian, ~5th c BCE), bell-rocket-belt (US, 1961), and doraemon-takecopter (JP, 1969), comprehensively places personal-VTOL flight in deep cross-cultural prior art base.
propulsion_details: Woven carpet capable of lifting one or more passengers and traveling rapidly through the air at the rider's mental command; vertical takeoff, vertical landing, and hover all canonical capabilities. Lift principle described as 'magic' but operationally identical to modern personal-VTOL concept.
sources: Alf Layla wa-Layla (One Thousand and One Nights), multiple manuscript traditions, 9th century CE onward.
Galland, Antoine. Les Mille et Une Nuits. Paris, 1704-1717 (12 volumes).
Lyons, Malcolm C. The Arabian Nights: Tales of 1,001 Nights. Penguin, 2008 (modern translation).

creator: Leonardo da Vinci
disclosure_citation: Leonardo da Vinci's Codex Atlanticus (1478-1519) and Codex on the Flight of Birds (1505) contain numerous sketches and engineering descriptions of ornithopters (flapping-wing machines) and the 'aerial screw' (helical lift surface) drafted approximately 1485-1505. The codices are housed at the Biblioteca Ambrosiana (Milan) and Royal Library of Turin; comprehensively published in critical facsimile editions.
ip_citations: Leonardo's manuscripts comprehensively in the public domain (creator died 1519)
prior_art_notes: Leonardo da Vinci's Codex Atlanticus and Codex on the Flight of Birds are the foundational European technical-engineering disclosure of vertical-flight aircraft concepts, predating Cornu (1907) by over 400 years. Establishes prior art for: (1) ornithopter (flapping-wing) architectural concept with explicit engineering sketches, (2) the 'aerial screw' as a helical-rotor vertical-lift device, (3) the coupled study of bird-flight aerodynamics with engineering replication. Comprehensively in the public domain. Combined with festo-smartbird (2011) and dune-ornithopter (1965), provides 600+ years of ornithopter and rotor-screw prior art.
propulsion_details: Multiple proposed mechanisms across Leonardo's notebooks: flapping wings powered by human muscle via cable-and-lever actuation; helical aerial screw for vertical lift; later sketches showing combined flapping-wing and tail-control surfaces. Anatomically-correct bird-flight studies informing the engineering proposals.
sources: Leonardo da Vinci. Codex Atlanticus. Critical edition: Marinoni, Augusto. Il Codice Atlantico. Giunti Barbera, multiple volumes 1973-1980.
Leonardo da Vinci. Codex on the Flight of Birds. Critical edition: Pedretti, Carlo. Codex on the Flight of Birds. Johnson Reprint Corporation, 1982.
Capra, Fritjof. The Science of Leonardo. Doubleday, 2007.

canonical_name: Comical History of the States and Empires of the Moon (Cyrano de Bergerac)
creator: Savinien de Cyrano de Bergerac
disclosure_citation: L'Autre Monde: ou les États et Empires de la Lune, by Savinien de Cyrano de Bergerac, posthumously published 1657 (Cyrano died 1655). Multiple fictional flight-machine designs proposed including: glass spheres evaporating dew (gas balloon precursor), magnet-powered flight, sequence of rocket-powered ascents (predating actual rocket-powered flight by 300 years). Critical editions: Madeleine Alcover (Champion 2000); Geoffrey Strachan (Oxford 1965).
ip_citations: Cyrano de Bergerac copyright long expired (creator died 1655)
prior_art_notes: Cyrano de Bergerac's Comical History of the Moon establishes 1657 French fictional prior art for staged rocket-propulsion vertical flight — remarkably predating Tsiolkovsky's rocket equation (1903) by 246 years and Robert Goddard's first liquid-fuel rocket (1926) by 269 years. Cyrano's literary description of sequential rocket-firework boosters achieving altitude is the earliest in literature. Combined with verne-albatross (1886, Verne's Albatross) and lana-terzi-vacuum-airship (1670), provides century-spanning French/Italian fictional prior-art anchors for vertical flight.
propulsion_details: Multiple fanciful mechanisms across the narrative including: dew-filled glass spheres lifting via solar-evaporation (gas-bag precursor), magnet-powered ascent via tossed magnet, sequential rocket-firework boosters propelling vertical ascent (an extraordinarily prescient anticipation of staged-rocket VTOL by exactly 312 years before Apollo 11). Cyrano's rocket-staging description is the earliest in literature.
sources: Cyrano de Bergerac, Savinien. L'Autre Monde, ou les États et Empires de la Lune. Paris, 1657 (posthumous).
Alcover, Madeleine (ed). Cyrano de Bergerac: L'Autre Monde. Champion Classiques, 2000.
Strachan, Geoffrey (trans). Cyrano de Bergerac: Voyages to the Moon and the Sun. Oxford University Press, 1965.

creator: Francesco Lana de Terzi
disclosure_citation: Francesco Lana de Terzi's Prodromo overo Saggio di alcune inventioni nuove premesso all'arte maestra (Brescia, 1670) describes a proposed lighter-than-air craft using four large evacuated copper spheres for lift. The first scientifically-rigorous proposal for a heavier-than-atmosphere lift mechanism in Western technical literature.
ip_citations: Lana de Terzi published prior to all modern copyright; comprehensively in the public domain
prior_art_notes: Francesco Lana de Terzi's vacuum airship (1670) is the foundational Italian technical-engineering proposal for a controllable lifting vehicle, predating the Montgolfier balloon (1783) by 113 years. Establishes Italian academic prior art for: (1) the concept of static-lift aerial vehicles via reduced-density gas, (2) scientific engagement with the engineering question 'how can a vehicle achieve neutral buoyancy in air?'. Although vacuum spheres are structurally impractical, Lana's analytical framework (compute lift force from displaced air mass) is the same framework all subsequent lighter-than-air craft use.
propulsion_details: Four evacuated copper spheres providing static lift (replacing air with vacuum to achieve net positive buoyancy); sail-and-oar propulsion in concept. The structural impossibility of the vacuum spheres withstanding atmospheric pressure was understood by 19th-century engineers but the concept persists as theoretical curiosity.
sources: Lana de Terzi, Francesco. Prodromo overo Saggio di alcune inventioni nuove premesso all'arte maestra. Brescia: Rizzardi, 1670.
Hodgson, John E. The History of Aeronautics in Great Britain. Oxford, 1924, chapter on vacuum-airship proposals.

creator: Jules Verne
disclosure_citation: Jules Verne. Maître du Monde (Master of the World). Hetzel et Cie, Paris, August 1904. Sequel to Robur le Conquérant (1886). The Terror is Robur's improved vehicle: capable of operation as automobile, boat, submarine, and aircraft.
ip_citations: Verne died 1905; copyright comprehensively expired
prior_art_notes: Verne's Terror is the foundational fictional disclosure of multi-modal (land + sea + air + underwater) transformer vehicle architecture — the 1904 anticipation of modern drive+fly transformers (Klein Vision AirCar, AeroMobil 4.0, PAL-V Liberty, ASKA A5, XPENG AeroHT modular) by over a century. Combined with Verne's earlier Albatross (1886, distributed electric multi-rotor lift), provides two distinct foundational fictional prior-art anchors from a single creator. Cited extensively in scholarship of multi-modal vehicle concepts.
propulsion_details: Multi-modal vehicle transitioning between road, boat, submarine, and aircraft modes; described as electrically-powered with high speeds achieved in all four media. The fictional anticipation of drive+fly+sail transformer architecture by 110+ years.
sources: Verne, Jules. Maître du Monde. Hetzel et Cie, Paris, 1904.
Butcher, William. Jules Verne: The Definitive Biography. Thunder's Mouth Press, 2006.
Standard scholarly editions, multiple publishers.

creator: James Cameron / Ron Cobb (concept design) / 20th Century Fox
disclosure_citation: Aliens, directed by James Cameron, theatrical release 1986-07-18 in the U.S. The UD-4L Cheyenne dropship designed by Ron Cobb, with engineering depth in the Aliens Colonial Marines Technical Manual (Lee Brimmicombe-Wood, HarperPrism 1995) and ongoing Alien franchise design materials.
ip_citations: 20th Century Studios / Disney copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: The Aliens UD-4L Cheyenne dropship establishes 1986 cinematic prior art for tilt-thruster military VTOL atmospheric drop-vehicle architecture. Ron Cobb's design materials and the Aliens Colonial Marines Technical Manual (1995) provide engineering-grade depictions including thrust vectoring schematics. Combined with thunderbird-2 (UK, 1965), macross-vf-1-veritech (JP, 1982), gundam-gaw (JP, 1979), and avatar-samson-scorpion (US, 2009), provides multi-decade cinematic anticipation of military tilt-thruster VTOL transport architecture.
propulsion_details: Atmospheric drop-vehicle with twin tilting wing-mounted thruster nacelles and additional ventral lift jets; depicted with realistic engineering detail including landing-gear deployment and weapons-station integration. Capable of vertical takeoff from Sulaco hangar bay, atmospheric entry, hover-and-fire, and vertical landing on rough terrain.
sources: Cameron, James (dir.). Aliens. 20th Century Fox, 1986.
Cobb, Ron. Concept art for Aliens, 1985-1986; published in Aliens production design books.
Brimmicombe-Wood, Lee. The Colonial Marines Technical Manual. HarperPrism, 1995.

aliases: 機動警察パトレイバー Helldiver
creator: Headgear / Yutaka Izubuchi (mecha design) / Sunrise / Bandai Visual
disclosure_citation: Patlabor: The Mobile Police (機動警察パトレイバー), original video animation first release 1988-04-25; theatrical film Patlabor: The Movie released 1989-04-13. The Helldiver mecha-carrier and other Patlabor flight machines documented in the Patlabor Mechanical Files (Bandai, multiple volumes) and Headgear design archives.
ip_citations: Headgear / Bandai Namco copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: The Patlabor Helldiver establishes 1989 Japanese fictional prior art for tilt-rotor cargo carrier architecture in cinematic depiction contemporaneous with V-22 Osprey first flight (1989). Yutaka Izubuchi's mecha design notes explicitly reference V-22 lineage. Combined with macross-vf-1-veritech (1982), gundam-gaw (1979), ghost-in-the-shell-tiltrotor (1989), and laputa-flapter (1986), provides comprehensive Japanese fictional prior-art base for tilt-rotor and rotor-craft architectures.
propulsion_details: Tilt-rotor / tilt-wing hybrid carrier capable of vertical takeoff, hover, transition to high-speed cruise, and air-deployment of smaller mecha (Patlabor units). Distinctive stub-wing tilt-rotor geometry with under-belly cargo bay. Yutaka Izubuchi's design draws explicitly on V-22 Osprey (1989) which was contemporaneous.
sources: Patlabor: The Movie (機動警察パトレイバー the Movie). Bandai Visual / Sunrise, 1989.
Patlabor Mechanical Files. Bandai, multiple volumes 1989-2010.
Headgear (Mamoru Oshii / Yutaka Izubuchi / Kazunori Itō / Akemi Takada / Masami Yūki) design archives.

creator: Frant Gwo / Liu Cixin (original story) / China Film Group
disclosure_citation: The Wandering Earth (流浪地球), directed by Frant Gwo, theatrical release 2019-02-05 in China; based on Liu Cixin's 2000 novella. Production design materials documented in The Art of The Wandering Earth (Renmin University Press, 2019). Sequel The Wandering Earth 2 (2023) expanded the depiction with detailed cargo-VTOL deployment scenes.
ip_citations: China Film Group / Liu Cixin copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: The Wandering Earth establishes 2019 Chinese cinematic prior art for heavy-cargo VTOL atmospheric transport in extreme-environment operation. Combined with the existing Chinese commercial eVTOL prior-art base (EHang, AutoFlight, AeroHT XPENG, TCab, Geely Aerofugia), establishes China's substantial multi-decade cultural and industrial VTOL footprint. Adds Chinese fictional anchor distinct from the Japanese-dominated Asian fictional prior-art base.
propulsion_details: Heavy-cargo VTOL transport vehicles operating in extreme post-apocalyptic conditions (frozen earth surface); fictional fusion-electric powerplant; depicted hovering deployment of heavy machinery to United Earth Government (UEG) Earth-engine sites.
sources: Gwo, Frant (dir.). The Wandering Earth (流浪地球). China Film Group, 2019.
Liu, Cixin. The Wandering Earth (流浪地球). Sichuan Science and Technology Press, 2000 (novella).
The Art of The Wandering Earth. Renmin University Press, 2019.

creator: Gene Roddenberry / Desilu Productions / Paramount Pictures / CBS
disclosure_citation: Star Trek: The Original Series, episode 'The Galileo Seven,' first aired 1967-01-05 on NBC. The Galileo shuttlecraft is the first Federation shuttlecraft depicted on-screen. Subsequent Star Trek series and films develop dozens of shuttlecraft variants with increasing engineering detail in the Star Trek: The Next Generation Technical Manual (Pocket Books, 1991) and successor reference works.
ip_citations: CBS / Paramount Star Trek copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: Star Trek shuttlecraft establish 60+ years of continuous fictional depiction of small passenger VTOL vehicles capable of atmospheric and orbital flight. The Star Trek: The Next Generation Technical Manual (Sternbach and Okuda, 1991) provides engineering-grade depictions including hover thrust ratings, atmospheric flight envelopes, and interior layout schematics. Combined with bladerunner-spinner (1982), fifth-element-flying-taxi (1997), star-wars (separate franchise), and iron-man-suit (1963), provides comprehensive multi-decade US/UK fictional prior-art base for compact-passenger VTOL transport.
propulsion_details: Compact passenger vehicle with multi-mode propulsion: impulse engines for atmospheric and orbital flight; warp nacelles for FTL travel; anti-gravity for vertical hover. Capable of atmospheric landing on any planetary surface, hover, and vertical takeoff. Production design across Star Trek franchise establishes detailed engineering schematics for multiple shuttlecraft variants (Type-1, Type-6, Type-9, etc.).
sources: Star Trek: The Original Series, NBC, 1966-1969.
Sternbach, Rick and Okuda, Michael. Star Trek: The Next Generation Technical Manual. Pocket Books, 1991.
Robinson, Ben. Star Trek Shipyards: Starfleet Ships. Eaglemoss, multiple volumes 2017-2024.

control_architecture: distributed cooperative control across docked modules; wireless inter-module state sharing; each module's rotor contribution computed for composite airframe stability
creator: ETH Zurich Institute for Dynamic Systems and Control (Raffaello D'Andrea group) / Robin Oung
disclosure_citation: Oung, Raymond and D'Andrea, Raffaello. 'The Distributed Flight Array.' IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA), Anchorage AK, 2010-05-03. Subsequent papers: Oung & D'Andrea, 'Modeling and Control of a Distributed Flight Array,' International Journal of Robotics Research 31(4), 2011. ETH Zurich IDSC project documented in open technical materials and demonstration videos.
ip_citations: ETH Zurich academic publication; design materials open under academic publication conventions
prior_art_notes: ETH Zurich's Distributed Flight Array (Oung & D'Andrea, ICRA 2010) is the foundational academic disclosure of modular mid-air-assembling multirotor architecture — predating UPenn's ModQuad by 8 years. Establishes prior art for: (1) the architectural concept of multiple small unit-modules that dock together to form a single composite multirotor airframe, (2) distributed cooperative control across physically-connected modules sharing wireless state, (3) the fundamental rotor-failure-reconfiguration property where loss of any module is tolerated by the composite. ETH publications are comprehensively in the academic public-domain prior-art base. Anticipates: ModQuad (2018, UPenn), and any future commercial eVTOL claim asserting novelty over modular mid-air docking architecture.
propulsion_details: Single-rotor hexagonal modular units that drive together on the ground as a fleet, dock into a planar array using magnetic edge connectors, and take off together as a coordinated multirotor airframe. Each module has a single lift propeller, ground-drive wheels, and edge-mounted connectors. The composite airframe lift, attitude, and position are all controlled cooperatively across modules sharing wireless control state.
sources: Oung, R. and D'Andrea, R. 'The Distributed Flight Array.' ICRA 2010.
Oung, R. and D'Andrea, R. 'Modeling and Control of a Distributed Flight Array.' IJRR 31(4), 2011.
ETH Zurich IDSC project archive, multiple demonstration videos.

control_architecture: distributed cooperative control with mid-air docking; vision-based alignment (ModQuad-Vi); reconfigurable composite control (ModQuad-RP)
creator: University of Pennsylvania GRASP Laboratory / David Saldaña / Mark Yim / Vijay Kumar
disclosure_citation: Saldaña, David; Gabrich, Bruno; Li, Guanrui; Yim, Mark; Kumar, Vijay. 'ModQuad: The Flying Modular Structure that Self-Assembles in Midair.' IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA), Brisbane Australia, 2018-05-21 to 2018-05-25. Multiple successor papers including ModQuad-Vi (ICRA 2019), ModQuad-DoF (IROS 2018), ModQuad-RP (RAL 2020). UPenn GRASP Laboratory open-source hardware designs at github.com/swarmslab.
ip_citations: UPenn academic publication; design materials open under academic publication and BSD/MIT licensing
prior_art_notes: ModQuad establishes the foundational disclosure of *mid-air-docking* modular multirotor architecture — quadrotors that fly individually, align in flight, dock together, and operate as a single composite airframe. Establishes prior art for: (1) mid-air physical docking of quadrotor modules into a single rigid airframe, (2) magnetic edge-connector docking architecture for in-flight assembly, (3) distributed control allocation across N modules of a composite multirotor with N scaling up or down at runtime, (4) the cuboid-frame protective geometry enabling collision-tolerant docking maneuvers. Combined with eth-distributed-flight-array (2010 precursor), comprehensively places modular-docking eVTOL architecture in academic public-domain prior art. **Critical for any future commercial eVTOL patent claim asserting novelty over modular reconfigurable multirotor or swarm-to-single-airframe transitions** — a likely future commercial direction for scalable cargo/passenger eVTOL.
propulsion_details: Cuboid-framed quadrotor units (Crazyflie-class avionics inside a protective cuboid frame with magnetic edge connectors); multiple modules can align in-flight, dock via passive magnetic alignment and active locking mechanism, then operate as a single composite multirotor. Distinct from ETH DFA by enabling mid-air docking (not just ground-stage assembly). Composite control allocation redistributes attitude commands across all docked modules' rotors.
sources: Saldaña, D., Gabrich, B., Li, G., Yim, M., Kumar, V. 'ModQuad: The Flying Modular Structure that Self-Assembles in Midair.' ICRA 2018.
Saldaña, D. et al. 'ModQuad-Vi: A Vision-Based Self-Assembling Modular Quadrotor.' ICRA 2019.
Gabrich, B. et al. 'Design and Control of ModQuad-DoF.' IROS 2018.
Li, G. et al. 'A Modular Aerial Robot Platform: ModQuad-RP.' IEEE RAL, 2020.
UPenn GRASP Laboratory ModQuad project archive, github.com/swarmslab.

control_architecture: full autonomy with mission-planner ground station; designed for BVLOS operations in unprepared environments
creator: Orb Aerospace / Skyways Inc
disclosure_citation: Skyways Inc founded ~2017 in Austin, Texas. Skyways A22 autonomous VTOL cargo aircraft publicly demonstrated for USMC Naval Air Systems Command (NAVAIR) starting 2019; awarded USMC Tactical Resupply Vehicle (TRV-150) contract 2020. Orb Aerospace product line including the Orb Nomad publicly disclosed via Air Force AFWERX program records, Skyways press materials, and U.S. defense contracting disclosures. Distinct architectural commitment to industrial / defense / emergency-response cargo missions rather than urban passenger air taxi.
ip_citations: Skyways / Orb Aerospace patent estate, multiple US filings 2017-2024
prior_art_notes: Orb Aerospace / Skyways establishes US prior art for the industrial / defense / emergency-response cargo eVTOL category — distinct architectural and operational commitment from urban passenger air taxi. The USMC TRV-150 contract (2020) is among the earliest production-track autonomous VTOL cargo deployments in U.S. military service. Establishes prior art for: (1) hybrid-electric autonomous cargo eVTOL specifically designed for unprepared / austere site operations, (2) the industrial-first eVTOL deployment thesis (cargo and defense use cases preceding consumer urban air taxi). Together with elroy-air-chaparral, pyka-pelican, sabrewing-rhaegal-a, volansi-vbat, wingcopter-198, dji-flycart-30, and amsl-vertiia, comprehensively places autonomous hybrid-electric cargo eVTOL architecture in 2017-2024 commercial prior art across US, DE, AU, and CN industrial lineages.
propulsion_details: Hybrid-electric powertrain combining combustion genset and battery for long-range autonomous cargo missions; multiple lift rotors plus separate cruise propulsor in lift+cruise architecture; designed for operation from austere / unimproved sites without runway or vertiport infrastructure.
sources: Skyways Inc press releases and product materials 2017-2024.
USMC NAVAIR / TRV-150 contract award records.
U.S. Air Force AFWERX program disclosures.
Orb Aerospace public technical materials.
https://go.skyways.com/ — corporate landing page.

creator: Convertawings Inc / D. H. Kaplan
disclosure_citation: Convertawings Model A Quadrotor first flight 1956 at Amityville NY; designed by D. H. Kaplan. The first quadrotor with full cyclic and collective pitch control on each of the four rotors. U.S. Army / Navy evaluated; program ended ~1960 for lack of orders. Documented in contemporary aviation press and rotorcraft histories.
ip_citations: Kaplan / Convertawings quadrotor patents, 1950s — all expired
prior_art_notes: Convertawings Model A is the foundational quadrotor with full cyclic + collective control on each rotor — a direct architectural precursor of modern quadrotor eVTOL. Establishes prior art for: (1) the H-frame four-rotor configuration with cross-shafted multi-engine drive, (2) attitude control via per-rotor cyclic pitch (the de Bothezat 1922 design used differential collective only). Convertawings' 1950s patents are long expired. Combined with de-bothezat-quadrotor (1922), curtiss-wright-vz-7 (1958), and piasecki-vz-8-airgeep (1958), comprehensively places quadrotor architecture in mid-20th-century public-domain prior art.
propulsion_details: Four lift rotors at the corners of an H-frame (two laterally on each side); two Continental engines driving all four rotors via cross-shafting (engine-out continued lift). Each rotor has both cyclic and collective pitch — the first quadrotor to control attitude via cyclic blade pitch rather than the de Bothezat-style differential collective.
sources: Liberatore, E.K. Helicopters Before Helicopters. Krieger, 1998.
Markman and Holder. Straight Up. Schiffer, 2000.
Aviation Week coverage of Convertawings Model A, 1956-1959.

canonical_name: UPenn cooperative grasping and transport with multiple quadrotors
control_architecture: distributed cooperative control of a rigidly-coupled multi-quadrotor composite; payload-grasp mechanism
creator: University of Pennsylvania GRASP Laboratory / Daniel Mellinger / Michael Shomin / Nathan Michael / Vijay Kumar
disclosure_citation: Mellinger, Daniel; Shomin, Michael; Michael, Nathan; Kumar, Vijay. 'Cooperative Grasping and Transport using Multiple Quadrotors.' International Symposium on Distributed Autonomous Robotic Systems (DARS), Lausanne, November 2010; published Springer Tracts in Advanced Robotics, 2013. UPenn GRASP Laboratory; demonstration videos widely circulated 2010-2012.
ip_citations: UPenn academic publication; open under academic conventions
prior_art_notes: UPenn's cooperative-grasping work (Mellinger, Shomin, Michael, Kumar, DARS 2010) is the closest conceptual ancestor of ModQuad within the GRASP Laboratory — multiple quadrotors rigidly coupling to a shared body and cooperatively controlling it as a single composite multirotor. Establishes prior art for: (1) rigid in-air coupling of N independent quadrotors into a single controlled composite, (2) distributed cooperative control allocation across the coupled set, (3) cooperative aerial transport / manipulation. Combined with eth-distributed-flight-array (2010) and upenn-modquad (2018), comprehensively places modular / cooperatively-coupled multirotor architecture in academic public-domain prior art.
propulsion_details: Multiple individual quadrotors rigidly attaching to a shared payload (via gripper mechanisms) and cooperatively controlling the combined system as a single composite multirotor. Each quadrotor's thrust contributes to the composite airframe; control is distributed but coordinated. Predates ModQuad by 8 years and is its closest conceptual ancestor within the GRASP Lab itself.
sources: Mellinger, D., Shomin, M., Michael, N., Kumar, V. 'Cooperative Grasping and Transport using Multiple Quadrotors.' DARS 2010 / STAR 2013.
Michael, N., Fink, J., Kumar, V. 'Cooperative manipulation and transportation with aerial robots.' Autonomous Robots 30(1), 2011.
UPenn GRASP Laboratory project archive.

control_architecture: autonomous fleet control with motion-capture localization; coordinated pick-and-place across multiple quadrotors
creator: ETH Zurich Gramazio Kohler Research / Raffaello D'Andrea (flying machine control) / FRAC Centre Orléans
disclosure_citation: Flight Assembled Architecture installation, FRAC Centre, Orléans, France, 2011-12-02 to 2012-02-19. ETH Zurich Gramazio Kohler Research (Fabio Gramazio, Matthias Kohler) with flying-machine control by Raffaello D'Andrea's group. Quadrotors autonomously assembled a 6-meter tower from 1,500 foam modules. Documented in academic publications and the book 'Flight Assembled Architecture' (Editions HYX, 2013).
ip_citations: ETH Zurich academic publication; open under academic conventions
prior_art_notes: ETH Zurich's Flight Assembled Architecture (Gramazio Kohler + D'Andrea, 2011-2012) is the foundational disclosure of autonomous aerial robots constructing load-bearing structures by coordinated assembly of modular components. Establishes prior art for: (1) coordinated multi-quadrotor fleet assembly of structures from modular building blocks, (2) the concept of on-demand aerially-assembled structures (directly relevant to 'a flying structure built on demand from individual modules'), (3) autonomous pick-and-place aerial manipulation at fleet scale. Combined with eth-distributed-flight-array (2010), upenn-cooperative-grasping (2010), and upenn-modquad (2018), comprehensively places modular-aerial-assembly architecture in academic public-domain prior art.
propulsion_details: Fleet of quadrotors equipped with gripper mechanisms, autonomously picking up lightweight foam modules and placing them to construct a tall structure under motion-capture localization. The quadrotors are the builders, not the structure — but the disclosure establishes the concept of aerial robots assembling load-bearing structures, directly relevant to any modular / on-demand-assembled eVTOL airframe claim.
sources: Willmann, J., Augugliaro, F., Cadalbert, T., D'Andrea, R., Gramazio, F., Kohler, M. 'Aerial Robotic Construction: Towards a New Field of Architectural Research.' International Journal of Architectural Computing 10(3), 2012.
Gramazio, F., Kohler, M., D'Andrea, R. (eds). Flight Assembled Architecture. Editions HYX, 2013.
Augugliaro, F. et al. 'The Flight Assembled Architecture installation: Cooperative construction with flying machines.' IEEE Control Systems Magazine 34(4), 2014.

canonical_name: University of Bologna Modular Multirotor Vehicle
control_architecture: composite control of under-actuated modules joined by a reconfigurable mechanism; achieves full actuation from individually-under-actuated parts
creator: University of Bologna CASY (Center for Research on Complex Automated Systems) / Roberto Naldi / Lorenzo Marconi
disclosure_citation: Naldi, Roberto; Marconi, Lorenzo et al. 'Modeling and Control of a Class of Modular Aerial Robots Combining Under-Actuated Air Vehicles With a Reconfigurable Mechanism.' IEEE Transactions on Robotics, subsequent to 2011 conference work. University of Bologna CASY group; papers in IEEE T-RO, IFAC, and ICRA 2011-2015.
ip_citations: University of Bologna academic publication; open under academic conventions
prior_art_notes: The University of Bologna's modular multirotor work (Naldi & Marconi, CASY group, ~2011-2015) is the Italian academic disclosure of modular-reconfigurable aerial robots combining under-actuated thrust modules with a reconfigurable connecting mechanism. Establishes Italian prior art for: (1) modular aerial vehicles where the composite achieves full actuation from individually-under-actuated parts, (2) reconfigurable inter-module mechanisms changing thrust-vector geometry. Together with the ETH and UPenn modular-aerial-robot lineages, places modular multirotor architecture in cross-national academic public-domain prior art.
propulsion_details: Modular aerial robot architecture combining under-actuated air-vehicle modules (each contributing thrust along a fixed direction) with a reconfigurable connecting mechanism that allows the composite to achieve full actuation; the relative orientation of modules can be changed to reorient thrust vectors. Italian academic disclosure of modular-reconfigurable multirotor with controllable composite dynamics.
sources: Naldi, R., Marconi, L. et al. 'Modeling and Control of a Class of Modular Aerial Robots.' IEEE Transactions on Robotics, 2011-2015 (multiple papers).
Naldi, R., Gentili, L., Marconi, L., Sala, A. 'Design and experimental validation of a nonlinear control law for a ducted-fan miniature aerial vehicle.' Control Engineering Practice, 2010.
University of Bologna CASY group publications archive.

control_architecture: centralized trajectory generation + onboard attitude control; motion-capture localization; coordinated formation flight and dynamic reconfiguration
creator: KMel Robotics (UPenn GRASP Laboratory spinout) / Alex Kushleyev / Daniel Mellinger / Vijay Kumar
disclosure_citation: Kushleyev, Alex; Mellinger, Daniel; Powers, Caitlin; Kumar, Vijay. 'Towards a swarm of agile micro quadrotors.' Robotics: Science and Systems (RSS), Sydney, 2012; published Autonomous Robots 35(4), 2013. The famous '20 nano quadrotors flying in formation' video was released by the UPenn GRASP Lab / KMel Robotics 2012-01-31 (viewed tens of millions of times). KMel Robotics later acquired by Qualcomm.
ip_citations: UPenn academic publication; KMel Robotics IP later assigned to Qualcomm
prior_art_notes: The KMel Robotics / UPenn GRASP nano-quadrotor swarm (Kushleyev, Mellinger, Powers, Kumar, RSS 2012) is the foundational public disclosure of large-scale agile multirotor swarm coordination — the famous '20 nano quadrotors' video. Establishes prior art for: (1) tightly-coordinated 3D formation flight of N independent multirotors, (2) dynamic in-flight reconfiguration of swarm formations, (3) coordinated passage through constrained openings while maintaining formation. Combined with intel-shooting-star (2016) and collmot-drone-show (2014), comprehensively places multi-drone coordinated-fleet control in pre-2017 prior art. The 'fleet acting as one system' concept central to modern modular/swarm eVTOL is anticipated here.
propulsion_details: Fleet of 20 nano-quadrotors (~73 g each) flying in tightly-coordinated 3D formations, executing figure-eights, grid reconfigurations, and passing through windows in formation. Centralized trajectory planning with onboard attitude control; motion-capture localization. The foundational public disclosure of large-scale agile multirotor swarm coordination.
sources: Kushleyev, A., Mellinger, D., Powers, C., Kumar, V. 'Towards a swarm of agile micro quadrotors.' RSS 2012 / Autonomous Robots 35(4), 2013.
UPenn GRASP Laboratory / KMel Robotics video, 'A Swarm of Nano Quadrotors,' 2012-01-31.

control_architecture: gimbal-decoupled attitude control inside a rotating protective cage; collision-tolerant flight
creator: EPFL Laboratory of Intelligent Systems (Dario Floreano) / Adrien Briod (later Flyability SA)
disclosure_citation: Briod, Adrien; Kornatowski, Przemyslaw; Zufferey, Jean-Christophe; Floreano, Dario. 'A Collision-resilient Flying Robot.' Journal of Field Robotics 31(4), 2014. EPFL Laboratory of Intelligent Systems; won the UAE Drones for Good Award 2015; commercialized as Flyability Elios via the Flyability SA spinout.
ip_citations: EPFL / Flyability patents on gimbaled protective-cage flying robots
prior_art_notes: EPFL's GimBall (Briod, Kornatowski, Zufferey, Floreano, JFR 2014) is the foundational disclosure of the gimbaled protective-cage flying-robot architecture — a freely-rotating outer cage that decouples collision impacts from the inner flight system, enabling collision-tolerant flight. Establishes prior art for: (1) rotating protective-cage airframe geometry, (2) collision-tolerant operation enabling contact-based maneuvers (including docking). The protective-cage concept is directly antecedent to ModQuad's cuboid-frame docking architecture. Commercialized as Flyability Elios for industrial confined-space inspection.
propulsion_details: Coaxial-rotor flying robot enclosed in a freely-rotating spherical carbon-fiber protective cage on a gimbal; the cage absorbs and decouples collision impacts, allowing the robot to bump into obstacles and continue flying. The protective-cage concept is the direct ancestor of ModQuad's cuboid frame and any collision-tolerant docking eVTOL architecture.
sources: Briod, A., Kornatowski, P., Zufferey, J.-C., Floreano, D. 'A Collision-resilient Flying Robot.' Journal of Field Robotics 31(4), 2014.
EPFL Laboratory of Intelligent Systems publications archive.
Flyability SA technical materials.

control_architecture: decentralized self-organizing flocking; GPS localization; inter-drone communication; choreographed formation light shows
creator: CollMot Robotics / CollMot Entertainment (Budapest, Hungary) / Gábor Vásárhelyi / Tamás Vicsek
disclosure_citation: CollMot Robotics founded 2014 in Budapest by Gábor Vásárhelyi (with roots in Tamás Vicsek's collective-motion research group at Eötvös Loránd University); decentralized outdoor drone-swarm flight published in Vásárhelyi, G. et al. 'Optimized flocking of autonomous drones in confined environments,' Science Robotics 3(20), 2018. CollMot Entertainment performs commercial drone-light shows worldwide.
ip_citations: CollMot Robotics patents on decentralized drone-swarm coordination, Hungarian / EU filings
prior_art_notes: CollMot Robotics establishes Hungarian prior art for decentralized outdoor drone-swarm coordination — distinct from motion-capture-based indoor swarms (UPenn nano-quad, Intel Shooting Star) by operating outdoors at scale with onboard GPS and decentralized self-organizing flocking. Rooted in Tamás Vicsek's foundational collective-motion physics (the Vicsek model, 1995). The Science Robotics 2018 paper (Vásárhelyi et al.) is a comprehensive disclosure. Adds Hungary to the eVTOL / aerial-robotics prior-art map. Together with kmel-nano-quadrotor-swarm and intel-shooting-star, places multi-drone coordinated-fleet control in continuous prior-art coverage.
propulsion_details: Fleet of GPS-localized outdoor quadrotors flying decentralized self-organizing flocking patterns and choreographed light-show formations; distinct from motion-capture-based indoor swarms by operating outdoors at scale with onboard GPS and inter-drone communication. Hungarian research lineage from Tamás Vicsek's foundational collective-motion physics work.
sources: Vásárhelyi, G., Virágh, C., Somorjai, G., Nepusz, T., Eiben, A.E., Vicsek, T. 'Optimized flocking of autonomous drones in confined environments.' Science Robotics 3(20), 2018.
Vicsek, T. et al. 'Novel type of phase transition in a system of self-driven particles.' Physical Review Letters 75(6), 1995.
CollMot Robotics / CollMot Entertainment technical materials.

control_architecture: single-operator centralized control of N-drone fleet; GPS localization; choreography-software fleet trajectory generation; the fleet flies as one controlled system
creator: Intel Corporation
disclosure_citation: Intel Shooting Star drone-light-show system publicly demonstrated 2016-11-04 (500-drone Guinness World Record, Krailling Germany); subsequently scaled to 1,218 drones (PyeongChang Olympics opening ceremony 2018-02-09) and 2,018+ drones (Folsom CA, 2018-07). Intel filed multiple patents on swarm light-show choreography and coordinated multi-drone control. Publicly disclosed via Intel press materials and Guinness World Records.
ip_citations: Intel patent estate on drone-swarm light shows and coordinated multi-drone control, multiple US filings 2015-2020
prior_art_notes: Intel Shooting Star establishes US commercial prior art for large-scale single-operator coordinated drone-fleet control — 500 drones (2016), 1,218 drones (PyeongChang 2018 Olympics), 2,018+ drones (2018). Establishes prior art for: (1) one operator controlling a fleet of N independent multirotors as a single coordinated system, (2) choreography-software-driven fleet trajectory generation at scale, (3) the 'fleet flies as one system' control paradigm directly relevant to any modular / swarm-coordinated eVTOL claim. Together with kmel-nano-quadrotor-swarm (2012) and collmot-drone-show (2014), comprehensively places coordinated-multi-drone control in 2012-2018 prior art across US academic, US commercial, and Hungarian commercial lineages.
propulsion_details: Fleet of purpose-built lightweight quadrotors (~330 g, foam-and-plastic, single onboard LED) flown in tightly-choreographed 3D formations from a single ground-station operator; GPS localization with centralized show choreography software ('one operator controls the fleet'). The Shooting Star Mini variant (~12 g, indoor) followed.
sources: Intel Corporation press releases on Shooting Star, 2016-2020.
Guinness World Records, 'Most unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) airborne simultaneously,' 2016-11-04 and 2018-07.
PyeongChang 2018 Olympic Winter Games opening ceremony, 2018-02-09.

control_architecture: standard quadrotor control after deployment; deployment is a discrete one-shot unfold
creator: EPFL Laboratory of Intelligent Systems (Dario Floreano) / Stefano Mintchev
disclosure_citation: Mintchev, Stefano; Floreano, Dario. 'A pocket sized foldable quadrotor for collaborative missions.' (and related papers). EPFL Laboratory of Intelligent Systems, 2017; published IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters and ICRA/IROS 2017-2019. Origami-inspired quadrotor that folds into a compact package for storage/transport and self-deploys for flight.
ip_citations: EPFL patents on foldable origami aerial structures
prior_art_notes: EPFL's origami folding drone (Mintchev & Floreano, 2017) establishes prior art for foldable / self-deploying multirotor airframes — arms that collapse into a compact transport package and deploy for flight. Establishes prior art for: (1) origami-inspired collapsible quadrotor arm architecture, (2) spring-loaded or motor-driven self-deployment. Together with uzh-foldable-drone (active mid-flight folding) and univ-tokyo-dragon (in-flight multilink transformation), comprehensively places morphing / folding aerial-robot architecture in academic public-domain prior art.
propulsion_details: Origami-folding quadrotor whose arms collapse into a compact rectangular package (fitting in a pocket) and self-deploy via spring-loaded or motor-driven unfolding when activated. Distinct from the UZH Foldable Drone (active mid-flight folding) — the EPFL design folds for storage/transport then deploys once before flight.
sources: Mintchev, S., Floreano, D. 'A pocket sized foldable quadrotor for collaborative missions.' Related EPFL LIS publications 2017-2019.
Mintchev, S., Daler, L., L'Eplattenier, G., Saint-Raymond, L., Floreano, D. 'Foldable and Self-Deployable Pocket Sized Quadrotor.' ICRA 2015.
EPFL Laboratory of Intelligent Systems publications archive.

control_architecture: autonomous; capsule transfers between ground and air modules; air module is a coaxial-octorotor lifting the docked capsule
creator: Airbus / Italdesign / Audi
disclosure_citation: Airbus Pop.Up modular ground-and-air mobility concept unveiled 2017-03-07 at the Geneva International Motor Show, by Airbus and Italdesign (Volkswagen Group / Audi subsidiary); Pop.Up Next (adding Audi as full partner) unveiled 2018-03-06 at Geneva. Documented in Airbus and Italdesign press materials and design patents. The system is a passenger capsule that docks to either a ground-vehicle skateboard module or a quadrotor air module.
ip_citations: Airbus / Italdesign / Audi Pop.Up design and utility patents, EU and PCT filings 2016-2020
prior_art_notes: Airbus Pop.Up / Pop.Up Next (Airbus + Italdesign + Audi, 2017-2018) is the foundational commercial disclosure of the modular-docking passenger-mobility concept: a passenger capsule that docks to either a ground-vehicle module or an air module (a large coaxial-octorotor frame), with the air module lifting the docked capsule mid-journey. Establishes prior art for: (1) modular passenger-capsule + interchangeable-propulsion-module architecture, (2) in-journey transfer of a passenger pod between ground and air carriers, (3) the multi-modal modular-mobility thesis. Together with xpeng-aeroht-modular (2024), bell-apt (2018), and the academic modular-aerial-robot lineage (eth-distributed-flight-array, upenn-modquad), comprehensively places modular-docking eVTOL architecture in commercial and academic prior art from 2010 forward.
propulsion_details: Modular three-piece system: (1) a passenger capsule (cabin pod), (2) a ground-vehicle 'skateboard' module with road wheels, (3) an air module — a large coaxial-octorotor frame. The capsule docks to whichever module is appropriate for the trip leg; the air module lifts the capsule away from the ground module mid-journey. Battery-electric throughout. The modular-docking passenger-mobility concept.
sources: Airbus press release, 'Pop.Up: the modular ground-and-air passenger concept vehicle system,' 2017-03-07.
Italdesign / Audi Pop.Up Next materials, Geneva Motor Show 2018.
Airbus / Italdesign Pop.Up design patents, EUIPO records.

control_architecture: real-time control allocation adapting to changing airframe morphology; servo-folding arms; vision-based gap detection and traversal
creator: University of Zurich Robotics and Perception Group (Davide Scaramuzza) / Davide Falanga / Kevin Kleber / Stefano Mintchev / Dario Floreano
disclosure_citation: Falanga, Davide; Kleber, Kevin; Mintchev, Stefano; Floreano, Dario; Scaramuzza, Davide. 'The Foldable Drone: A Morphing Quadrotor That Can Squeeze and Fly.' IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters 4(2), 2018 / presented ICRA 2019. University of Zurich Robotics and Perception Group (with EPFL LIS collaboration). A quadrotor that actively folds its arms in flight to change its morphology and pass through narrow gaps.
ip_citations: UZH / EPFL patents on morphing quadrotor airframes
prior_art_notes: UZH's Foldable Drone (Falanga, Kleber, Mintchev, Floreano, Scaramuzza, RAL 2018 / ICRA 2019) is the foundational disclosure of in-flight repeatable airframe morphing — a quadrotor that actively folds its arms to change its geometry mid-flight (X → H → O shape) and adapts its control allocation in real time. Establishes prior art for: (1) servo-actuated repeatable in-flight airframe reconfiguration, (2) real-time control-allocation adaptation to changing rotor geometry, (3) morphology-driven obstacle traversal. Together with univ-tokyo-dragon (multilink transformation) and epfl-folding-drone (one-shot deployment), comprehensively places morphing aerial-robot architecture in academic public-domain prior art — directly relevant to any modular or reconfigurable eVTOL claim involving in-flight geometry change.
