ip_citations: JetPack Aviation Speeder / JB-series patents, US filings 2010-2024
prior_art_notes: The JetPack Aviation Speeder establishes US prior art for the jet-turbine hover-bike — a motorcycle-style straddle VTOL with four vectored jet turbines and fly-by-wire stabilization, no exposed rotors. Establishes prior art for: (1) jet-turbine-powered hover-bike (distinct from open-rotor hover bikes), (2) vectored-turbine lift and translation control for a personal VTOL, (3) heavy-fuel turbine personal VTOL. JetPack Aviation also developed the JB-series jetpacks (turbojet jetpacks flown 2015-2024) — together with bell-rocket-belt (1961), williams-x-jet (1982), gravity-industries-jet-suit (2017), martin-aircraft-jetpack (2008), solotrek-xfv (2001), aerofex-aero-x (2008), hoversurf-s3 (2017), and the historical personal-platform anchors (Hiller VZ-1, de Lackner HZ-1, Chrysler VZ-6), comprehensively places personal-VTOL platform and hover-bike architecture in deep prior art across rocket / ducted-fan / turbofan / turbojet / open-rotor / jet-turbine variants.
propulsion_details: A motorcycle-style straddle vehicle with four jet turbines (kerosene/diesel/Jet-A capable) arranged so their thrust can be vectored for lift and translation; fly-by-wire stabilization (the rider just steers); no exposed rotors (turbojet/turbofan exhaust instead). A jet-turbine hover-bike — distinct from the open-rotor hover bikes (Aerofex, Hoversurf, Malloy) and from the personal jet-suit (Gravity, JetPack Aviation's own JB-series).
sources: JetPack Aviation Speeder / JB-series technical materials 2015-2024.
U.S. Air Force AFWERX and U.S. Army contract records (JetPack Aviation).
JetPack Aviation patent filings, USPTO.

canonical_name: Malloy Aeronautics Hoverbike / US Army JTARV
control_architecture: fly-by-wire stabilization; rider steers (crewed variant) or autonomous (cargo TRV-150 variant); differential-thrust attitude control
creator: Malloy Aeronautics (United Kingdom; founder Chris Malloy, Australian) — later partnered with SURVICE Engineering / U.S. Army; acquired by BAE Systems 2024
disclosure_citation: Malloy Aeronautics publicly unveiled the Hoverbike (a quadcopter straddle vehicle) at Paris Air Show 2014; partnered with SURVICE Engineering for the U.S. Army's Joint Tactical Aerial Resupply Vehicle (JTARV) programme 2015; the cargo variant became the TRV-150 Tactical Resupply UAS for the U.S. Marine Corps. Malloy Aeronautics acquired by BAE Systems 2024. Documented in Malloy materials and U.S. Army/USMC contract records.
ip_citations: Malloy Aeronautics Hoverbike patents, UK / US / PCT filings 2014-2024
prior_art_notes: Malloy Aeronautics' Hoverbike / U.S. Army JTARV / USMC TRV-150 establishes prior art for the four-ducted-rotor hover-bike-and-cargo-quad family that reached U.S. military service — the crewed Hoverbike and the uncrewed TRV-150 cargo variant on a shared four-ducted-rotor airframe. Establishes prior art for: (1) ducted-rotor hover-bike architecture, (2) crewed-and-uncrewed variants on a common airframe, (3) operational military deployment of a hover-bike-class platform. Acquired by BAE Systems 2024. Together with aerofex-aero-x (2008), hoversurf-s3 (2017), jetpack-aviation-speeder (2021), and the historical personal-platform anchors, comprehensively places hover-bike architecture in commercial and military prior art. (Note: the Malloy TRV-150 is closely related to but distinct from the Skyways/Orb Aerospace TRV-150 lineage — both used the 'TRV-150' designation for USMC tactical-resupply VTOL.)
propulsion_details: A quadcopter in a motorcycle-straddle configuration (or, in the cargo variant, an uncrewed quad cargo platform) with four ducted electric rotors at the corners; fly-by-wire stabilization; battery or hybrid-electric power. The crewed Hoverbike and the uncrewed TRV-150 cargo variant share the same four-ducted-rotor airframe. An open-rotor (actually ducted-rotor) hover-bike / cargo quad that reached U.S. military service.
sources: Malloy Aeronautics Hoverbike / TRV-150 technical materials 2014-2024.
U.S. Army JTARV / USMC TRV-150 Tactical Resupply UAS contract records.
BAE Systems acquisition of Malloy Aeronautics, 2024.

control_architecture: rider control with stabilization; wheel-hub-mounted tilting micro-turbojets for lift and translation
creator: Lazareth Auto-Moto (Annecy, France) / Ludovic Lazareth
disclosure_citation: Lazareth Auto-Moto (a French custom-vehicle maker) unveiled the LMV 496 'La Moto Volante' — a motorcycle whose four wheels each contain a JetCat micro-turbojet that swivels downward for VTOL flight — in September 2019; tethered hover demonstrations 2021. A French jet-turbine flying motorcycle / hover-bike. Documented in Lazareth materials and motoring/aviation press.
ip_citations: Lazareth Auto-Moto LMV 496 design patents, French / EU filings
prior_art_notes: The Lazareth LMV 496 establishes French prior art for the four-micro-turbojet flying motorcycle — a road motorcycle whose four wheels each house a tilting micro-turbojet, swiveling downward for VTOL flight. Establishes prior art for: (1) wheel-integrated tilting-turbojet VTOL drive, (2) the jet-turbine flying-motorcycle / drive+fly hover-bike hybrid. Together with jetpack-aviation-speeder (2021, jet-turbine hover-bike), hoversurf-s3 (2017, open-rotor hover-bike), aerofex-aero-x (2008), and malloy-aeronautics-hoverbike (2014, ducted-rotor), comprehensively places hover-bike / flying-motorcycle architecture in cross-national prior art. Adds another French entry alongside the Eurocopter X3 / Airbus RACER / Flying Whales / SNECMA / Cyrano / Verne French VTOL heritage.
propulsion_details: A motorcycle (the Lazareth LM 847 'Maсерати-V8' custom bike) whose four wheels each house a JetCat axial-flow micro-turbojet; on the ground it drives on the wheels, then the wheel hubs tilt the turbojets downward and the bike lifts off as a four-jet VTOL. A four-micro-turbojet hover-bike / flying motorcycle — the French entry in the personal-jet-VTOL design space.
sources: Lazareth Auto-Moto LMV 496 technical materials and press releases 2019-2024.
Motoring and aviation press coverage of the Lazareth flying motorcycle 2019-2021.

creator: Carter Aviation Technologies (Wichita Falls, Texas) / Jay Carter Jr
disclosure_citation: Carter Aviation Technologies' CarterCopter Technology Demonstrator first flight September 1998; achieved 'Mu-1' (rotor advance ratio of 1.0 — flying as fast as the rotor tips advance, the holy grail of slowed-rotor compounds) on 2005-06-17, a documented first. The Carter PAV (Personal Air Vehicle) followed (2011). Carter's slowed-rotor technology was later licensed to Jaunt Air Mobility for an eVTOL. Documented in Carter Aviation technical papers and patents.
ip_citations: Carter Aviation Technologies Slowed Rotor/Compound (SR/C) patents, multiple US filings 1994-2020
prior_art_notes: The CarterCopter / Carter PAV (Carter Aviation, 1998-2011) is the foundational modern disclosure of 'Slowed Rotor/Compound' (SR/C) technology — a high-inertia rotor that spins fast for jump-takeoff (stored kinetic energy), then is dramatically slowed in cruise while a wing carries lift and a pusher provides thrust, reaching rotor advance ratios > 1.0 (the 'Mu-1' milestone, documented 2005). Establishes prior art for: (1) high-inertia jump-takeoff rotor, (2) dramatic rotor-RPM reduction in cruise (slowed-rotor compound), (3) the wing-offloads-rotor-in-cruise compound architecture. Carter's SR/C patents were licensed to Jaunt Air Mobility for an eVTOL. Together with autogyro/compound anchors (Cierva 1923, Fairey Gyrodyne 1947, Fairey Rotodyne 1957), karem-ar40 (2008, optimum-speed rotor), jaunt-air-mobility (2019), and overair-butterfly (2020), comprehensively places slowed-rotor / variable-RPM-rotor compound architecture in public-domain and licensed prior art.
propulsion_details: A compound rotorcraft using 'Slowed Rotor/Compound' (SR/C) technology: a high-inertia two-blade rotor that spins fast for jump-takeoff (stored kinetic energy), then is slowed dramatically in cruise (rotor RPM cut to ~50-100 of nominal) while a wing carries the lift and a pusher propeller provides thrust; reaching rotor advance ratios > 1.0. The foundational slowed-rotor-compound disclosure — directly licensed into the eVTOL era by Jaunt Air Mobility.
sources: Carter Aviation Technologies SR/C technical papers, AHS/VFS Forums 1998-2020.
Carter, Jay Jr. Slowed Rotor/Compound patents, USPTO.
FAI / AHS documentation of the CarterCopter Mu-1 milestone, 2005-06-17.

creator: Jaunt Air Mobility LLC (Montreal, Canada / Texas, USA) — subsidiary of AIRO Group; licensed Carter Aviation SR/C technology
disclosure_citation: Jaunt Air Mobility (founded 2019, headquartered in Montreal with Texas operations, a subsidiary of the AIRO Group) unveiled the Journey eVTOL — a slowed-rotor compound electric VTOL using 'ROSA' (Reduced rOtor Speed Aircraft) technology licensed from Carter Aviation — in 2019; selected for the U.S. Air Force AFWERX Agility Prime programme 2020. Documented in Jaunt Air Mobility materials, AFWERX records, and AIRO Group SEC filings (NASDAQ: AIRO).
ip_citations: Jaunt Air Mobility ROSA patents (licensed Carter Aviation SR/C technology), US filings 2019-2024
prior_art_notes: Jaunt Air Mobility's Journey eVTOL establishes Canadian/US prior art for the slowed-rotor-compound electric VTOL — a single large rotor that spins fast for jump-takeoff then slows dramatically in cruise while a wing carries lift and electric pushers provide thrust ('ROSA', licensed from Carter Aviation). A distinct eVTOL architecture from the multirotor and tilt-rotor approaches. Establishes prior art for: (1) slowed-rotor-compound electric VTOL, (2) jump-takeoff stored-inertia rotor in an electric eVTOL, (3) the wing-offloads-slowed-rotor-in-cruise eVTOL configuration. Together with cartercopter (1998, the SR/C origin), karem-ar40 (2008, optimum-speed rotor), and overair-butterfly (2020, OSR eVTOL), comprehensively places slowed/variable-RPM-rotor compound eVTOL architecture in prior art. Adds another Canadian entry alongside Horizon Aircraft and the Avro Canada Avrocar heritage.
propulsion_details: An electric compound rotorcraft: a single large four-blade rotor that spins fast for vertical takeoff/landing (jump-takeoff, stored inertia), then is slowed to ~50 of nominal RPM in cruise while a fixed wing carries the lift and four wing-mounted electric pusher propellers provide forward thrust; lithium-ion battery. The eVTOL realization of Carter Aviation's SR/C slowed-rotor compound technology — distinct architecture from both the multirotor and the tilt-rotor eVTOL approaches.
sources: Jaunt Air Mobility Journey technical materials 2019-2024.
U.S. Air Force AFWERX Agility Prime contract records (Jaunt).
AIRO Group SEC filings (NASDAQ: AIRO).

creator: Overair Inc (Santa Ana, California) — Karem Aircraft spinoff; partnered with Hanwha Systems (Korea)
disclosure_citation: Overair (spun out of Karem Aircraft in 2020, with investment from Hanwha Systems of South Korea) unveiled the Butterfly eVTOL — a tilt-rotor eVTOL using Karem's 'Optimum Speed Tilt-Rotor' (OSTR) technology (large slow-turning prop-rotors with continuously-variable RPM) — in October 2020. Documented in Overair materials, Hanwha Systems disclosures, and U.S. Air Force AFWERX Agility Prime records.
ip_citations: Overair / Karem Aircraft OSTR / OSR patents, multiple US filings 2008-2024
prior_art_notes: Overair's Butterfly establishes prior art for the optimum-speed tilt-rotor eVTOL — a tilt-rotor eVTOL with four very large, slow-turning prop-rotors whose RPM is continuously varied with flight regime ('OSTR', from Karem Aircraft's OSR technology), giving low disk loading for quiet, efficient hover. Distinct from the small-fast-prop tilt-rotor eVTOLs (Joby S4, TCab E20) by the large-slow-prop approach. Establishes prior art for: (1) continuously-variable-RPM tilt-rotor in an eVTOL, (2) very-low-disk-loading large slow prop-rotors for quiet hover, (3) the OSR-applied-to-eVTOL configuration. Hanwha Systems (Korea) partnership ties this to the Korean eVTOL ecosystem (Hyundai Supernal, Plana). Together with karem-ar40 (2008, OSTR origin), cartercopter (1998), and jaunt-air-mobility (2019), comprehensively places variable-RPM / slowed-rotor / optimum-speed-rotor eVTOL architecture in prior art.
propulsion_details: A tilt-rotor eVTOL with four very large, slow-turning prop-rotors (low disk loading for quiet hover and high efficiency) whose RPM is continuously varied as a function of flight regime ('Optimum Speed Tilt-Rotor', from Karem Aircraft's OSR technology); the rotors tilt from vertical to horizontal for cruise; electric distributed propulsion. Distinct from the small-fast-prop tilt-rotor eVTOLs (Joby S4) by the large-slow-prop optimum-speed-rotor approach.
sources: Overair Butterfly technical materials 2020-2024.
Hanwha Systems (Korea) Overair investment disclosures.
U.S. Air Force AFWERX Agility Prime contract records (Overair).
Karem Aircraft / Overair OSR / OSTR patents, USPTO.

creator: Doroni Aerospace (Fort Lauderdale, Florida) / Doron Merdinger
disclosure_citation: Doroni Aerospace (founded 2016 by Doron Merdinger) unveiled the H1 two-seat personal eVTOL in 2022; first crewed test flight 2023-05; received an FAA Special Airworthiness Certificate (Experimental) 2023; developing the H1-X for FAA Part 23 certification. Designed to fit in a two-car garage and operate from driveways under (intended) Part 103 ultralight or LSA rules. Documented in Doroni materials and FAA filings.
ip_citations: Doroni Aerospace patent estate, US filings 2017-2024
prior_art_notes: Doroni H1 establishes US prior art for the garage-storable enclosed-rotor two-seat personal eVTOL — eight ducted lift rotors housed within the airframe body (not on exposed booms), small fixed wings, car-like footprint, driveway operation. Establishes prior art for: (1) fully-enclosed-within-body lift rotors on a personal eVTOL, (2) garage-storable / driveway-operable two-seat personal eVTOL form factor. Together with pivotal-blackfly, jetson-one, lift-aircraft-hexa, alef-model-a, and the historical personal-platform anchors, comprehensively places small-personal eVTOL architecture in prior art.
propulsion_details: An enclosed two-seat personal eVTOL with eight ducted electric lift rotors (housed within the airframe body, not on exposed booms — a fully-enclosed-rotor design for safety and a car-like footprint), plus small fixed wings for cruise lift; lithium-ion battery; fly-by-wire with simplified controls. A garage-storable, driveway-operable two-seat enclosed-rotor eVTOL.
sources: Doroni Aerospace H1 / H1-X technical materials 2022-2024.
FAA Special Airworthiness Certificate dossier (Doroni H1).

creator: teTra Aviation Corporation (Tokyo, Japan) / Tasuku Nakai
disclosure_citation: teTra Aviation Corporation (founded 2018 in Tokyo, an outgrowth of GoFly Prize-competing students) revealed the Mk-3 personal eVTOL (2020, GoFly Prize 'Pratt & Whitney Disruptor Award' winner) and subsequently the production Mk-5 single-seat personal eVTOL (2021); first crewed flights and FAA Special Airworthiness Certificate process underway. Documented in teTra materials and GoFly Prize records.
ip_citations: teTra Aviation patent estate, Japanese / US filings 2018-2024
prior_art_notes: teTra Mk-5 establishes Japanese prior art for the high-rotor-count single-seat personal eVTOL — a fixed-wing airframe with 32 small distributed lift rotors for hover redundancy plus wing lift for cruise efficiency. A GoFly Prize-derived design. Establishes prior art for: (1) very-high-rotor-count (~32) personal eVTOL with fixed wings, (2) the GoFly Prize lineage of personal-flight designs. Together with skydrive-sd-05 (JP, multirotor air taxi), aska-a5 (JP/US, drive+fly), honda-evtol (JP, hybrid), and the Japanese fictional VTOL anchors, deepens Japan's eVTOL footprint. Together with lift-aircraft-hexa (US, 18-rotor) and ehang-eh216 (CN, 16-rotor), places high-rotor-count personal/small eVTOL architecture in prior art.
propulsion_details: A single-seat personal eVTOL with a large number of small distributed lift rotors (the Mk-5 uses 32 fixed-pitch electric rotors mounted on tandem fixed wings — a 'fixed-wing-with-many-distributed-lift-rotors' design for hover redundancy and cruise efficiency); lithium-ion battery; designed for the personal-recreational segment. The Japanese entry in the high-rotor-count personal eVTOL space.
sources: teTra Aviation Mk-3 / Mk-5 technical materials 2019-2024.
GoFly Prize competition records (teTra Mk-3, Pratt & Whitney Disruptor Award).

creator: Plana Aerospace Inc (Seongnam, South Korea)
disclosure_citation: Plana Aerospace (founded 2021 in South Korea by Braden Kim) unveiled the CP-01 hybrid-electric eVTOL in 2022; sub-scale prototype flight tests 2023-2024; targeting Korean (MOLIT) and FAA certification for regional air mobility. Documented in Plana materials and Korean Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport (MOLIT) records.
ip_citations: Plana Aerospace patent estate, Korean / US / PCT filings 2021-2024
prior_art_notes: Plana Aero CP-01 establishes Korean prior art for the hybrid-electric regional eVTOL — a lift+cruise eVTOL with a turbine-genset-electric hybrid powertrain for ~500 km range, targeting intercity routes. Distinct from the urban-air-taxi-focused Korean peer Hyundai Supernal (battery-electric, 100 km). Establishes Korean depth in the hybrid-electric eVTOL space alongside Honda (JP), Zuri (CZ), Elroy Air (US), and AMSL Vertiia (AU). Together with hyundai-supernal-sa2, the Hanwha-Systems-backed overair-butterfly, and (fictionally) space-sweepers-victory, deepens Korea's eVTOL footprint.
propulsion_details: A lift+cruise eVTOL with eight tilting electric lift rotors and a pusher cruise propeller, powered by a turbine-genset-electric hybrid powertrain for ~500 km range (distinguishing Plana from pure-battery Korean and global peers); designed for regional intercity routes. The Korean hybrid-electric regional-eVTOL entry.
sources: Plana Aerospace CP-01 technical materials 2022-2024.
Korean MOLIT (Ministry of Land, Infrastructure and Transport) UAM programme records.

creator: Sarla Aviation (Bangalore, India)
disclosure_citation: Sarla Aviation (founded 2023 in Bangalore by Adrian Schmidt, Rakesh Gaonkar, and Shivam Chauhan; named after Sarla Thakral, India's first woman pilot) unveiled the Shunya eVTOL air taxi in November 2023, with production targeted for ~2028 in Indian cities (Bangalore, Mumbai, Delhi, Pune); sub-scale prototype work 2024. Documented in Sarla materials and Indian DGCA engagement records.
ip_citations: Sarla Aviation patent estate, Indian / PCT filings 2023-2024
prior_art_notes: Sarla Aviation Shunya establishes additional Indian prior art for the urban-air-taxi multirotor eVTOL — a five-seat eVTOL designed for short hops in dense Indian cities, the second major Indian eVTOL OEM after ePlane. Establishes Indian depth alongside eplane-company-e200 (IN, lift+cruise) and the Indian fictional flight anchor (Krrish). Together with ehang-eh216 (CN), volocopter-volocity (DE), and the global multirotor air-taxi cluster, places urban-air-taxi multirotor eVTOL architecture in prior art across US, EU, China, India, Japan, Korea, and Brazil lineages.
propulsion_details: A four-passenger (plus pilot) eVTOL air taxi with eight electric lift rotors, designed for short urban hops in dense Indian cities; lithium-ion battery; intended to be priced competitively with premium ground ride-hailing. The second major Indian eVTOL OEM (after ePlane).
sources: Sarla Aviation Shunya technical materials 2023-2024.
Indian DGCA (Directorate General of Civil Aviation) UAM engagement records.

creator: New Horizon Aircraft Ltd (Lindsay, Ontario, Canada)
disclosure_citation: New Horizon Aircraft (Canadian, founded 2013, public on NASDAQ as HOVR via SPAC merger 2024) unveiled the Cavorite X5 hybrid eVTOL in 2021; 50%-scale prototype first conventional flight 2023-11 and first full transition 2024-11. The Cavorite uses a 'fan-in-wing' design — fourteen lift fans embedded in the wings, covered by sliding panels in cruise so the aircraft flies as a conventional fixed-wing. Documented in Horizon Aircraft materials and SEC filings (named for H.G. Wells's anti-gravity material 'Cavorite').
ip_citations: New Horizon Aircraft Cavorite patents, Canadian / US filings 2013-2024
prior_art_notes: Horizon Aircraft Cavorite X5 establishes Canadian prior art for the modern 'fan-in-wing' hybrid eVTOL — fourteen electric lift fans embedded in the wing structure, covered by sliding panels in cruise so the wing becomes a clean conventional wing with a single pusher. Establishes prior art for: (1) modern panel-covered fan-in-wing eVTOL, (2) hybrid-electric fan-in-wing architecture, (3) the wing-as-clean-wing-in-cruise design. Directly descended in concept from vanguard-omniplane (1959) and ryan-xv-5-vertifan (1964). Together with these and lilium-jet (ducted-fan-array), aurora-lightningstrike-xv-24a (tilt-wing ducted-fan), and bell-nexus-4ex (tilt-duct), comprehensively places ducted-fan / fan-in-wing eVTOL architecture in prior art. Adds Canadian depth alongside Jaunt Air Mobility and the Avro Canada Avrocar heritage.
propulsion_details: A fixed-wing aircraft with fourteen electric lift fans embedded in the wing structure (seven per wing), covered by sliding panels that close in cruise so the wing becomes a clean conventional wing and a single rear pusher provides thrust; a turbine-genset-electric hybrid powertrain. The modern 'fan-in-wing' eVTOL — directly descended in concept from the Vanguard Omniplane (1959) and Ryan XV-5 Vertifan (1964) fan-in-wing VTOL programs. Adds Canadian eVTOL depth.
sources: New Horizon Aircraft Cavorite X5 technical materials 2021-2024.
New Horizon Aircraft SEC filings (NASDAQ: HOVR).

control_architecture: fly-by-wire with race-tuned flight envelope; remote-piloted (Mk3) or crewed (Mk4); aggressive maneuvering control
creator: Alauda Aeronautics / Airspeeder (Adelaide, Australia) / Matthew Pearson
disclosure_citation: Alauda Aeronautics (founded 2016 in Adelaide by Matthew Pearson) developed the Airspeeder racing eVTOL — the Mk3 (uncrewed remote-piloted racer, first flew 2021, raced in the 'EXA Series' 2022) and the Mk4 (crewed racer, unveiled 2022, designed for the manned Airspeeder Grand Prix). The world's first eVTOL racing series. Documented in Alauda/Airspeeder materials and Australian CASA filings.
ip_citations: Alauda Aeronautics / Airspeeder patent estate, Australian / PCT filings 2016-2024
prior_art_notes: Alauda Aeronautics' Airspeeder (Mk3 uncrewed 2021, Mk4 crewed 2022) establishes Australian prior art for the racing eVTOL — a high-power-to-weight single-seat (or uncrewed) octocopter designed for close-formation pylon racing, the world's first eVTOL racing series. Establishes prior art for: (1) racing-tuned eVTOL flight envelope and aggressive-maneuvering control, (2) the racing-eVTOL operational concept, (3) crewed/uncrewed variants on a racing airframe. Together with amsl-vertiia (AU, hydrogen passenger eVTOL), deepens Australia's eVTOL footprint. The racing-eVTOL niche is a distinct application alongside passenger air taxi, cargo, personal, and military eVTOL — and Airspeeder is its foundational disclosure.
propulsion_details: A single-seat (Mk4) or uncrewed (Mk3) racing octocopter — eight electric lift rotors in coaxial pairs on four arms, very high power-to-weight ratio for aggressive maneuvering; the Mk4 adds a hydrogen-fuel-cell range-extender concept; carbon-fiber monocoque; designed for close-formation pylon racing. The racing-eVTOL niche — distinct operational envelope (aggressive maneuvering, close formation) from passenger/cargo eVTOL.
sources: Alauda Aeronautics / Airspeeder Mk3 / Mk4 technical materials 2017-2024.
Airspeeder EXA Series race records 2022.
Australian CASA (Civil Aviation Safety Authority) Airspeeder filings.

creator: George Lucas / Lucasfilm / Industrial Light & Magic / Ralph McQuarrie (concept design)
disclosure_citation: Star Wars (later Episode IV: A New Hope), directed by George Lucas, U.S. theatrical release 1977-05-25 — featuring Luke Skywalker's X-34 landspeeder, which hovers and travels on 'repulsorlift' anti-gravity drive. Subsequent Star Wars films and the Expanded Universe develop an enormous catalogue of repulsorlift vehicles (speeder bikes, airspeeders, repulsor-tanks, sail barges) with detailed engineering depictions in the Star Wars Technical Manuals, Cross-Sections books, and the Star Wars Encyclopedia. Concept designs by Ralph McQuarrie and ILM.
ip_citations: Lucasfilm / Disney Star Wars copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: Star Wars repulsorlift vehicles (1977-present) are the canonical fictional disclosure of anti-gravity / repulsorfield VTOL — vehicles that hover effortlessly with no rotors, jets, or wings, with separate thrusters for horizontal propulsion. Establishes prior art for the anti-grav VTOL concept (the `fictional_repulsor` architecture). The Star Wars Cross-Sections, Technical Manuals, and Encyclopedia provide decades of detailed engineering depictions. Filed against any patent claim asserting novelty over the basic concept of 'a vehicle that hovers via a field interacting with gravity, with separate thrusters for translation,' Star Wars repulsorlift provides a dated, detailed fictional anticipation from 1977 forward. Combined with bttf-2-hoverboard (1989), arabian-nights-flying-carpet (~9th c), and pushpaka-vimana (~5th c BCE), comprehensively places anti-grav / field-lift VTOL in deep fictional prior art.
propulsion_details: 'Repulsorlift' drive — a fictional anti-gravity / repulsorfield technology that pushes against a planet's gravitational field to hover at low altitude (typically a few centimeters to a few kilometers); separate ion/turbine thrusters provide horizontal propulsion. Vehicles hover effortlessly with no rotors, no jets, no wings — the canonical fictional 'anti-grav VTOL' depiction. The Star Wars Cross-Sections and Technical Manuals provide schematic-grade depictions of repulsorlift coil arrangements.
sources: Lucas, George (dir.). Star Wars / Episode IV: A New Hope. Lucasfilm / 20th Century Fox, 1977.
Reynolds, David West et al. Star Wars: Incredible Cross-Sections (and Complete Cross-Sections). DK Publishing, multiple volumes 1998-2019.
Sansweet, Stephen J. et al. The Star Wars Encyclopedia. Del Rey, multiple editions.
McQuarrie, Ralph; concept art for Star Wars, 1975-1983.

canonical_name: Hoverboard and flying DeLorean (Back to the Future Part II)
creator: Robert Zemeckis / Bob Gale / Universal Pictures / ILM
disclosure_citation: Back to the Future Part II, directed by Robert Zemeckis, U.S. theatrical release 1989-11-22 — depicting the (fictional 'Mattel') hoverboard, a skateboard-without-wheels that hovers on a repulsorfield, and the DeLorean DMC-12 with a 'hover conversion' (the wheels fold flat and rotate to act as repulsor pads) for flying in the year 2015. Production design by Rick Carter; visual effects by ILM. Documented in Back to the Future production materials and the official Back to the Future trilogy reference books.
ip_citations: Universal Pictures / Amblin Back to the Future copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: The Back to the Future Part II hoverboard and flying DeLorean (1989) establish the most-recognized fictional personal repulsorlift device (the hoverboard) and a fictional drive+fly transformer with anti-grav conversion (the DeLorean's folding-wheel-repulsor-pad 'hover conversion'). Establishes prior art for: (1) personal anti-grav hover platform (the hoverboard — kinesthetically controlled, fails over water without extra power), (2) drive+fly transformer with wheels that fold/rotate to become lift devices (the DeLorean conversion — anticipating, in repulsor form, the Lazareth LMV 496's tilting-wheel-turbojets and the Alef Model A's whole-body-rotation). Together with star-wars-repulsorlift (1977), arabian-nights-flying-carpet, pushpaka-vimana, and the real drive+fly transformer cluster, places anti-grav VTOL and folding-wheel drive+fly architecture in fictional prior art.
propulsion_details: The hoverboard: a wheel-less skateboard that hovers a few centimeters above the ground on a fictional repulsorfield (does not work over water — 'unless you've got POWER!'); rider controls it kinesthetically like a skateboard. The flying DeLorean: a 'hover conversion' kit makes the wheels fold flat and rotate 90° to act as repulsor pads, lifting the car for flight; 'Mr. Fusion' power. Both are anti-grav / repulsorlift fictional VTOL — the hoverboard being the most-recognized fictional personal repulsor device in popular culture.
sources: Zemeckis, Robert (dir.). Back to the Future Part II. Universal Pictures / Amblin, 1989.
Klastorin, Michael. Back to the Future: The Ultimate Visual History. Harper Design, 2015.
Back to the Future trilogy production design materials, Universal/Amblin archives.

canonical_name: Flying taxi and police cruisers (The Fifth Element)
creator: Luc Besson / Gaumont / Columbia Pictures / Jean-Claude Mézières and Jean Giraud 'Moebius' (concept design)
disclosure_citation: The Fifth Element, directed by Luc Besson, premiered 1997-05-07 (Cannes) / wide release May 1997 — depicting a 23rd-century New York with multi-level flying traffic: Korben Dallas's yellow flying taxi, NYPD flying cruisers, and many other air vehicles, all hovering and flying on a fictional drive. Concept design by French comic artists Jean-Claude Mézières (creator of Valérian) and Jean Giraud ('Moebius'). Documented in The Fifth Element production design materials and the comics-art retrospectives of Mézières and Moebius.
ip_citations: Gaumont / Columbia Pictures / Mézières / Moebius copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: The Fifth Element (1997) is the canonical cinematic disclosure of the dense-multi-level-flying-traffic urban-air-mobility future — compact passenger and police air vehicles hovering and flying in layered urban airspace — designed by the French BD masters Jean-Claude Mézières (Valérian) and Jean Giraud ('Moebius'). Establishes French fictional prior art for: (1) the urban-air-mobility traffic vision (the exact vision modern eVTOL companies pitch — Joby, Archer, Lilium, Volocopter, Eve all invoke 'flying-car-filled cities'), (2) compact-passenger air vehicles operating in dense layered urban airspace, (3) the integrated-into-the-cityscape vertiport/skylane concept. Together with bladerunner-spinner (1982), jetsons-aerocar (1962), kin-dza-dza-pepelats (1986), star-trek-shuttlecraft (1966), and the Mézières/Moebius BD lineage, comprehensively places the urban-air-mobility fictional prior-art base (directly relevant to the operational-concept claims that the entire modern eVTOL industry rests on).
propulsion_details: Compact passenger and police air vehicles that hover at any altitude in dense multi-level urban traffic and fly horizontally; the depicted lift mechanism is fictional (no visible rotors or jets in normal operation — anti-grav-style hover) though some vehicles show retractable thrusters. The canonical 'flying-car-filled future city' depiction, designed by the French BD masters Mézières and Moebius — the most-cited fictional reference for the urban-air-mobility traffic vision that modern eVTOL companies invoke.
sources: Besson, Luc (dir.). The Fifth Element. Gaumont / Columbia Pictures, 1997.
Mézières, Jean-Claude; concept art for The Fifth Element, 1991-1996 (and the Valérian et Laureline BD series, 1967-2010).
Giraud, Jean ('Moebius'); concept art for The Fifth Element, 1991-1996.
The Fifth Element production design retrospectives, multiple.

creator: Joseph Kosinski / Universal Pictures / Daniel Simon (vehicle design)
disclosure_citation: Oblivion, directed by Joseph Kosinski, U.S. theatrical release 2013-04-10 — featuring the 'Bubbleship', a small two-seat VTOL reconnaissance/maintenance craft with a transparent bubble cockpit and twin tilting rotor/fan pods. Vehicle designed by Daniel Simon (former Bugatti designer; also designed the Tron Legacy Light Cycle). Documented in Oblivion production design materials and Daniel Simon's published design portfolios.
ip_citations: Universal Pictures Oblivion copyright on the depiction; Daniel Simon design copyright; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: The Oblivion Bubbleship (Daniel Simon design, 2013) is among the most engineering-detailed fictional small-VTOL designs — a two-seat tilt-rotor with a panoramic bubble cockpit, designed by ex-Bugatti designer Daniel Simon with documented tilt-mechanism and rotor-geometry notes. Establishes prior art for the two-seat bubble-canopy tilt-rotor configuration. Frequently cited as a visual reference for two-seat eVTOL concepts (the bubble canopy + twin tilting pods aesthetic). Together with bladerunner-spinner (1982), the Macross/Ghost-in-the-Shell/Patlabor Japanese tilt-rotor fictional anchors, avatar-samson-scorpion (2009), and the real tilt-rotor cluster (Bell XV-3 1955 onward), places fictional and real tilt-rotor architecture in comprehensive prior art.
propulsion_details: A small two-seat VTOL with a panoramic transparent bubble cockpit and twin pods (each with a rotor/fan) mounted on short stub-wings that tilt from vertical (hover) to horizontal (cruise); depicted with realistic engineering detail (Daniel Simon's design notes specify the tilt mechanism and rotor geometry). One of the most engineering-detailed fictional small-VTOL designs, frequently cited as a visual reference for two-seat eVTOL concepts.
sources: Kosinski, Joseph (dir.). Oblivion. Universal Pictures, 2013.
Simon, Daniel. Cosmic Motors and subsequent design portfolios (Bubbleship design notes).
Oblivion production design materials, Universal archives.

creator: Neill Blomkamp / WETA Workshop / TriStar Pictures / QED International
disclosure_citation: District 9, directed by Neill Blomkamp (South African), U.S./South African co-production, theatrical release 2009-08-14 — featuring alien ('Prawn') dropships and a large mothership hovering over Johannesburg, plus a powered exosuit ('exo') with VTOL-augmented jumps. WETA Workshop (New Zealand) executed the design. Documented in District 9 production materials and the WETA Workshop design retrospectives.
ip_citations: TriStar Pictures / Sony District 9 copyright on the depiction; WETA Workshop design copyright; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: District 9's alien dropship and powered exosuit (Blomkamp / WETA Workshop, 2009) establish a South African fictional VTOL anchor — a heavy industrial dropship that descends, hovers over urban terrain, and lands vertically, plus a VTOL-augmented powered exosuit. Adds South African fictional VTOL prior art alongside pegasus-universal-aerospace (ZA, real VTOL business-jet concept). Together with the global cinematic VTOL-transport fictional anchors (Aliens Cheyenne 1986, Avatar Samson/Scorpion 2009, Thunderbird 2 1965, Mobile Suit Gundam Gaw 1979, Patlabor Helldiver 1989, Wandering Earth 2019), and with iron-man-suit / krrish-flight-suit / SoloTrek / Williams X-Jet (personal-VTOL exoskeleton/platform anchors), places military/cargo VTOL transport and powered-exosuit VTOL in comprehensive cross-national fictional + real prior art. Adds the deepest African representation in the corpus's fictional layer (with the Egyptian 1001 Nights flying carpet).
propulsion_details: A heavy, industrial-looking alien dropship that descends vertically from the mothership, hovers over urban terrain, and lands vertically; the depicted lift is fictional (anti-grav-style hover with visible thruster augmentation in some shots). Distinctively grimy, industrial, used-future aesthetic (Blomkamp/WETA). The film also features a powered exosuit capable of VTOL-augmented leaps. Adds a South African fictional VTOL anchor.
sources: Blomkamp, Neill (dir.). District 9. TriStar Pictures / QED International, 2009.
WETA Workshop District 9 design materials and retrospectives.

canonical_name: The Victory and orbital tugs (Space Sweepers)
creator: Jo Sung-hee / Bidangil Pictures / Netflix (distribution)
disclosure_citation: Space Sweepers (Korean: 승리호 Seungriho), directed by Jo Sung-hee, released 2021-02-05 (Netflix) — Korea's first big-budget space science-fiction film, featuring 'The Victory', a debris-collecting spaceship, and various orbital and atmospheric craft including VTOL-capable tugs and shuttles. Documented in Space Sweepers production materials and Korean film-industry coverage.
ip_citations: Bidangil Pictures / Netflix Space Sweepers copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: Space Sweepers (Jo Sung-hee, 2021) — Korea's first big-budget space-SF film — establishes a Korean fictional VTOL anchor with 'The Victory' (a debris-collecting ship capable of atmospheric VTOL landing) and various orbital/atmospheric VTOL tugs and shuttles. Adds Korean fictional VTOL prior art alongside the real Korean eVTOL OEMs (Hyundai Supernal, Plana, Hanwha-backed Overair). Together with the global fictional VTOL-craft anchors, deepens the corpus's Asian fictional layer (Japan: Doraemon, Macross, Patlabor, Ghost in the Shell, Laputa, Nausicaä, Mobile Suit Gundam; China: Wandering Earth; India: Krrish; Korea: Space Sweepers).
propulsion_details: Various craft in a 2092-set future: 'The Victory' (a beat-up debris-collecting ship capable of atmospheric entry and VTOL landing), orbital tugs, and shuttles; the depicted lift uses fictional fusion/ion thrusters with vectoring for VTOL. Korea's first major space-SF film, with a grimy used-future aesthetic comparable to Blade Runner and District 9. Adds a Korean fictional VTOL anchor.
sources: Jo, Sung-hee (dir.). Space Sweepers (승리호). Bidangil Pictures / Netflix, 2021.
Space Sweepers production design materials and Korean film-industry coverage.

creator: Sir George Cayley
disclosure_citation: Sir George Cayley, 'On Aerial Navigation' — convertiplane / 'Aerial Carriage' sketch published in Mechanics' Magazine, London, 1843-04-08, showing a heavier-than-air craft with multiple horizontal lifting rotors that could be reconfigured into 'aeroplane' propellers for horizontal cruise. The earliest documented engineering proposal for a convertible-rotor VTOL aircraft in modern technical literature. Documented in Mechanics' Magazine archives and Cayley's collected papers.
ip_citations: Cayley died 1857; comprehensively in the public domain
prior_art_notes: Sir George Cayley's 1843 'Aerial Carriage' sketch is the earliest documented engineering proposal for a convertiplane / transition-VTOL aircraft in modern technical literature — multiple horizontal lifting rotors for vertical takeoff that reconfigure into horizontal propellers for cruise. Establishes UK prior art predating all subsequent VTOL engineering disclosures by 64+ years (Cornu 1907, de Bothezat 1922, Pescara 1922). Published in Mechanics' Magazine 1843, comprehensively in the public domain. Filed against any patent claim asserting novelty over the basic convertiplane concept (rotors that change function between vertical and horizontal flight), this 1843 disclosure is foundational anticipating prior art. Cayley is recognized as the father of aerodynamics.
propulsion_details: Sketch depicts an airframe with four large horizontal lifting rotors for vertical takeoff plus two propellers for horizontal cruise; the lifting rotors are intended to be reconfigured ('shut up') into horizontal propellers during cruise. The earliest published engineering proposal for a transition-VTOL aircraft.
sources: Cayley, Sir George. 'On Aerial Navigation.' Mechanics' Magazine, 1843-04-08.
Pritchard, J. Laurence. Sir George Cayley, the Inventor of the Aeroplane. Max Parrish, 1961.
Royal Aeronautical Society, Cayley papers archive.

creator: Gustave de Ponton d'Amécourt
disclosure_citation: Vicomte Gustave de Ponton d'Amécourt designed and built a series of model helicopters from 1861-1863 (clockwork-driven, later steam) — the first aluminium model 'hélicoptère' was demonstrated 1863 at the Académie des Sciences, Paris; d'Amécourt coined the modern French word 'hélicoptère' (from Greek helix + pteron). The earliest named-as-helicopter engineering disclosure. Documented in French Académie des Sciences papers and contemporary aeronautical press.
ip_citations: d'Amécourt died 1888; long in the public domain
prior_art_notes: Ponton d'Amécourt's 1861-1863 models are the earliest documented coaxial counter-rotating helicopter engineering disclosures — and the etymological origin of the word 'helicopter'. Establishes French prior art predating Cornu (1907 first manned free-flight rotorcraft, also French) by 44 years. The d'Amécourt → Cornu → Pescara → Oehmichen → SNECMA → Eurocopter → Airbus French helicopter lineage spans 160+ years. Comprehensively in the public domain.
propulsion_details: Coaxial counter-rotating rotors driven first by a clockwork spring, later by a small steam engine; the model achieved short vertical lifts but never sustained free flight at scale. The earliest engineering-documented helicopter design and the source of the word 'helicopter'.
sources: Académie des Sciences (Paris) papers on d'Amécourt helicopter models, 1861-1863.
Liberatore, E.K. Helicopters Before Helicopters. Krieger, 1998.

creator: Enrico Forlanini
disclosure_citation: Enrico Forlanini's steam-powered helicopter model rose to ~13 m altitude and hovered for ~20 seconds in 1877 in Milan — the first powered heavier-than-air craft of any kind to rise vertically and stay aloft under its own power. Documented in Forlanini's papers, the Italian Aeronautical Society archives, and contemporary European technical press.
ip_citations: Forlanini died 1930; long in the public domain
prior_art_notes: Forlanini's 1877 steam helicopter model is the first powered heavier-than-air craft of any kind to rise vertically and sustain flight — 26 years before the Wright Flyer, 30 years before Cornu's first manned free-flight rotorcraft. Establishes Italian prior art for: (1) the first sustained powered vertical-takeoff flight of any kind, (2) coaxial counter-rotating rotor lift at model scale. Forlanini went on to design successful dirigible airships. Comprehensively in the public domain.
propulsion_details: Coaxial counter-rotating rotors driven by a small high-pressure steam engine; the model weighed ~3.5 kg and achieved ~13 m altitude and ~20 s flight time. The first powered heavier-than-air craft to fly under its own power — predating the Wright Flyer (1903) by 26 years and the Cornu manned helicopter (1907) by 30 years.
sources: Forlanini, Enrico. Papers and reports on the steam helicopter, Italian Aeronautical Society archives, 1877-1885.
Liberatore, E.K. Helicopters Before Helicopters. Krieger, 1998.

creator: Jacob Christian Ellehammer
disclosure_citation: Jacob Ellehammer's helicopter briefly lifted off in Copenhagen 1912-09-13 (some sources say 1912-08-13) — one of the earliest manned-craft brief lift-offs in Europe, predating the first Cornu free-flight reports by reinterpretation. Documented in Danish Aeronautical Society archives and Ellehammer's patent filings.
ip_citations: Ellehammer died 1946; long in the public domain
prior_art_notes: Jacob Ellehammer's 1912 helicopter establishes Danish prior art in the foundational helicopter-pioneer era — one of the earliest manned VTOL craft to lift off, contemporaneous with Sikorsky's first Russian-Imperial helicopter attempts (1909-1910) and predating de Bothezat's 1922 first sustained manned multirotor hover by a decade. Adds Denmark (DK) to the global VTOL prior-art map. Ellehammer was also a pioneer of European powered aeroplane flight (first European powered hop, 1906).
propulsion_details: Two large counter-rotating coaxial rotors arranged as upper and lower disks driven by a 36 hp Ellehammer-designed radial engine; the craft hopped briefly but never achieved sustained controlled flight. Ellehammer also built the first powered aeroplane to fly in Europe (1906). Adds Denmark to the global helicopter-pioneer footprint.
sources: Ellehammer, Jacob. Helicopter patent and engineering filings, Danish/European, 1910s.
Danish Aeronautical Society archives, Ellehammer collection.
Liberatore, E.K. Helicopters Before Helicopters. Krieger, 1998.

creator: Henry Berliner / Emile Berliner / Berliner Aircraft Company
disclosure_citation: Henry Berliner (son of Emile Berliner, inventor of the gramophone) demonstrated his first practical helicopter to the U.S. Army at College Park MD on 1922-06-16 — the same year as the de Bothezat quadrotor and the Pescara coaxial helicopter. The Berliner machine made tethered hops and short free flights through 1925. Documented in U.S. Army Air Service reports, Smithsonian National Air and Space Museum collections (a Berliner is preserved at College Park), and Berliner family papers.
ip_citations: Henry Berliner / Berliner Aircraft Company patents, 1910s-1920s — long expired
prior_art_notes: Henry Berliner's 1922 helicopter is the parallel American disclosure to the de Bothezat quadrotor and Pescara coaxial — three independent 1922 manned-multirotor disclosures across the U.S., Argentina/France, and France. Establishes prior art for: (1) lateral side-by-side twin-rotor architecture in 1922 (predating Focke-Wulf Fa 61 by 14 years), (2) the parallel-track development of practical rotorcraft across multiple national prior-art bases. The U.S. Army Air Service evaluated the Berliner alongside the de Bothezat. Berliner's patents are long expired.
propulsion_details: Two large counter-rotating rotors mounted on lateral outriggers (side-by-side configuration, similar to the later Focke-Wulf Fa 61 architecture) plus a smaller vertical-axis tail rotor for trim; driven by a 220 hp Le Rhône rotary engine. A parallel American 1922 disclosure to the de Bothezat (1922) and Pescara (1922) machines — three independent 1922 manned-multirotor disclosures, demonstrating the rotorcraft design space was being actively explored across the US, Argentina/France, and France in the same year.
sources: U.S. Army Air Service Berliner helicopter test reports, College Park MD, 1922-1925.
Smithsonian National Air and Space Museum, Berliner helicopter collection (Paul E. Garber facility, College Park).
Berliner, Henry / Emile Berliner family papers, Library of Congress.

creator: Cierva Autogiro Company / Cunliffe-Owen Aircraft (UK)
disclosure_citation: Cierva W.11 Air Horse first flight 1948-12-08 at Eastleigh, Hampshire. The largest helicopter in the world at the time — three large rotors arranged in tandem (forward, port-aft, starboard-aft) driven by a single Rolls-Royce Merlin engine via cross-shafting. Cancelled after a fatal crash 1950. Documented in Cierva / Cunliffe-Owen archives and UK Ministry of Supply reports.
ip_citations: Cierva Autogiro Company / Cunliffe-Owen Air Horse patents — expired
prior_art_notes: The Cierva W.11 Air Horse (1948) is the foundational disclosure of the three-rotor heavy-lift helicopter architecture — three large rotors arranged at the corners of a triangle, cross-shafted to a single engine. The largest helicopter in the world at the time, predating the Soviet Mil V-12 (1968, two-rotor lateral) and the Sikorsky CH-53E (1974). Establishes UK prior art for triangular-three-rotor lift architecture — a distinct multirotor configuration from the X-frame quadrotor (de Bothezat 1922), tandem (Piasecki 1945), lateral (Focke-Wulf Fa 61 1936, Berliner 1922), and coaxial (Pescara 1922) lineages. Cierva's post-Juan-de-la-Cierva company carried the Cierva-name autogyro/rotorcraft tradition into the post-war heavy-lift era.
propulsion_details: Three large lift rotors arranged at the corners of a triangle (one forward, two aft); a single Rolls-Royce Merlin 24 engine drives all three via cross-shafting. The Cierva Autogiro Company's post-Juan-de-la-Cierva successor design — a three-rotor heavy-lift helicopter architecture distinct from the contemporaneous single-main-rotor (Sikorsky) and tandem-rotor (Piasecki) lineages. The largest helicopter in the world for several years.
sources: Cierva Autogiro Company / Cunliffe-Owen W.11 Air Horse program archives.
UK Ministry of Supply Air Horse test reports.
Brooks, Peter W. Cierva Autogiros. Smithsonian, 1988.

canonical_name: Cavorite (The First Men in the Moon, H.G. Wells)
creator: H.G. Wells (Herbert George Wells)
disclosure_citation: H.G. Wells. The First Men in the Moon. Serialized in Cosmopolitan and The Strand Magazine December 1900 to August 1901; book publication November 1901 (UK, George Newnes) and 1901 (US, Bowen-Merrill). The novel introduces 'Cavorite', a fictional substance that blocks gravity — used to construct a spherical spacecraft that rises vertically and travels to the Moon. The foundational literary disclosure of an anti-gravity material as the basis for VTOL/space flight; the directly-named ancestor of Horizon Aircraft's Cavorite X5 eVTOL (2021).
ip_citations: Wells died 1946; novel comprehensively in the public domain
prior_art_notes: H.G. Wells's Cavorite (The First Men in the Moon, 1901) is the foundational literary disclosure of an anti-gravity material as the basis for vertical-takeoff flight — predating Star Wars repulsorlift (1977) by 76 years. Specifically named as the inspiration for Horizon Aircraft's Cavorite X5 eVTOL (2021), making this entry the direct fictional ancestor of a current commercial eVTOL trademark. Comprehensively in the public domain (Wells died 1946). Combined with star-wars-repulsorlift, bttf-2-hoverboard, arabian-nights-flying-carpet, and pushpaka-vimana, places anti-gravity / fictional_repulsor architecture in continuous literary prior art from ~5th c BCE through modern cinema.
propulsion_details: Fictional 'Cavorite' is a metal alloy that blocks gravity (the narrative is precise: it is opaque to gravitational attraction the way ordinary metals are opaque to light). A craft coated in Cavorite rises by closing shutters to expose Cavorite to space (becoming gravitationally lighter) and steers by opening shutters in chosen directions. The most-canonical fictional anti-gravity material — predating Star Wars repulsorlift (1977) by 76 years and Cities in Flight spindizzy (1955) by 54 years.
sources: Wells, H.G. The First Men in the Moon. The Strand Magazine, serialized December 1900 to August 1901; book editions George Newnes (UK) and Bowen-Merrill (US), 1901.
Bergonzi, Bernard. The Early H.G. Wells: A Study of the Scientific Romances. Manchester University Press, 1961.

creator: James Blish
disclosure_citation: James Blish. Earthman, Come Home (the first 'Cities in Flight' novel; published Putnam 1955-03, expanded from earlier 'Okie' novelettes published in Astounding Science Fiction 1950-1953). The novel introduces the 'spindizzy' (formally the Dillon-Wagoner Graviton Polarity Generator) — an anti-gravity drive that lifts entire cities into space. A foundational mid-20th-century literary anti-grav disclosure. Documented in Blish's Cities in Flight tetralogy and SF scholarship.
ip_citations: Blish died 1975; Cities in Flight remains under copyright but the engineering description is unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: James Blish's spindizzy (Cities in Flight, 1955) is a foundational mid-20th-century literary anti-grav disclosure — an anti-gravity drive used at urban scale to lift entire cities, with mass-scaling efficiency. Establishes US literary prior art for: (1) heavy-lift anti-grav VTOL at structural scale, (2) gravity-polarity manipulation as a propulsion concept. Together with hg-wells-cavorite (1901, molecular anti-grav material), star-wars-repulsorlift (1977), and bttf-2-hoverboard (1989), places literary anti-grav prior art in continuous cross-decade coverage.
propulsion_details: The 'spindizzy' (Dillon-Wagoner Graviton Polarity Generator) manipulates gravity at the scale of large structures (entire cities) to provide both lift and propulsion; the field intensity scales with mass enclosed, so larger structures gain efficiency. The earliest extensively-developed literary anti-grav drive supporting heavy-lift VTOL at urban scale — directly relevant to any commercial eVTOL claim involving heavy-lift anti-grav (a recurring speculative patent-application area).
sources: Blish, James. Earthman, Come Home. Putnam, 1955.
Blish, James. Cities in Flight (tetralogy: They Shall Have Stars, A Life for the Stars, Earthman Come Home, The Triumph of Time). Avon, 1970 omnibus.
Blish, James. Okie stories, Astounding Science Fiction, 1950-1953.

creator: Pipistrel (Slovenia; acquired by Textron 2022)
disclosure_citation: Pipistrel (a Slovenian light-aircraft maker, the manufacturer of the Velis Electro — the first type-certified electric aeroplane) unveiled the Nuuva V300 hybrid VTOL cargo aircraft 2019-10-15; Pipistrel acquired by Textron 2022-04. The Nuuva V300 is intended as a heavy-cargo lift+cruise hybrid-electric eVTOL. Documented in Pipistrel/Textron materials and EASA filings.
ip_citations: Pipistrel / Textron patent estate, multiple Slovenian / EU / US filings 2015-2024
prior_art_notes: Pipistrel Nuuva V300 establishes Slovenian prior art for the hybrid-electric heavy-cargo eVTOL — eight electric lift rotors plus a single combustion-driven cruise pusher, designed for ~460 kg cargo over ~2,500 km range. Adds Slovenia (SI) to the global eVTOL OEM map. Pipistrel's parent company is Textron (also owner of Bell Textron) — the Pipistrel-Textron-Bell axis creates an integrated tilt-rotor + hybrid-cargo + electric-fixed-wing eVTOL family. Together with elroy-air-chaparral, sabrewing-rhaegal-a, wingcopter-198, pyka-pelican, amsl-vertiia, autoflight-prosperity-i, dji-flycart-30, and orb-aerospace-nomad, comprehensively places hybrid-electric cargo eVTOL architecture in commercial prior art across nine national/regional lineages.
propulsion_details: Eight electric lift rotors mounted on twin booms (four per side) plus a single combustion-driven pusher cruise propeller (a Rotax 915iS engine); designed for ~460 kg cargo payload and ~2,500 km range. Pipistrel's specific architectural choice — eight lift + single combustion-pusher — was selected for fuel-burn efficiency in cruise. Adds Slovenia (SI) to the global eVTOL OEM footprint and is now under Textron ownership alongside Bell.
sources: Pipistrel Nuuva V300 technical materials 2019-2024.
Textron acquisition of Pipistrel, 2022-04 disclosures.
EASA SC-VTOL public docket (Pipistrel).

creator: Volocopter GmbH (Bruchsal, Germany)
disclosure_citation: Volocopter VoloDrone publicly demonstrated 2019-10-30 at the Volocopter facility in Bruchsal, Germany — a heavy-cargo unmanned variant of the Volocopter 18-rotor multirotor architecture. Subsequently demonstrated for DB Schenker cargo logistics and at the Volocopter Hamburg test campaign. Documented in Volocopter press materials and EASA filings.
ip_citations: Volocopter GmbH patent estate (shared with VoloCity), German / EU / PCT filings
prior_art_notes: Volocopter VoloDrone establishes German prior art for the cargo-variant-of-passenger-multirotor pattern — unmanned heavy-cargo operations on the same 18-rotor airframe as the VoloCity passenger eVTOL, with up to 200 kg payload. Establishes prior art for: (1) shared-airframe passenger/cargo eVTOL family architecture, (2) underslung cargo and swappable container variants of a multirotor passenger eVTOL. Adds the cargo dimension to the Volocopter prior-art lineage (alongside the passenger volocopter-vc1 / volocopter-volocity).
propulsion_details: Eighteen brushless DC motors on a circular ring (same multirotor architecture as the VoloCity passenger eVTOL, but in unmanned cargo configuration) carrying a payload up to 200 kg under a slung load or in a swappable cargo container; battery-electric. The cargo variant of the Volocopter passenger eVTOL platform — establishes the cargo-variant-of-passenger-platform pattern that Wisk, Eve, and others may follow.
sources: Volocopter VoloDrone press materials and demonstrations 2019-2024.
DB Schenker / Volocopter cargo logistics partnership records.

control_architecture: fly-by-wire; tilt-rotor transition control
creator: Joby Aviation (Santa Cruz, California)
disclosure_citation: Joby S2 design publicly disclosed by JoeBen Bevirt and Joby Aviation August 2015 (technical white paper and Vertical Flight Society Forum presentation); first untethered transition flight 2017-08. The single-pilot predecessor that established Joby Aviation's six-rotor tilt-rotor architecture later scaled up for the S4 production design. Documented in Joby technical white papers and AHS/VFS Forum presentations 2015-2018.
ip_citations: Joby Aviation S2 / S4 patent estate priority chain, US filings 2014-2020
prior_art_notes: Joby Aviation's S2 (2015) is the architectural prototype for Joby's six-tilt-rotor distributed-electric-propulsion eVTOL — establishing the 4-wing + 2-stabilator tilt-rotor configuration two years before the public S4 unveil (joby-s4, 2018) and anchoring the Joby Aviation patent estate priority chain. The 2015 disclosure makes Joby's architectural choices public prior art before any patent dates that depend on the S4 disclosure date. Important for invalidity-contention purposes: any Joby patent claiming priority later than 2015 against S2-disclosed elements has prior art from Joby's own earlier disclosure.
propulsion_details: Six tilting electric prop-rotors arranged in the same 4-wing + 2-stabilator layout that scaled up to the production S4; single-pilot configuration, much smaller than the S4 (~1/3 the gross weight). The architectural prototype for Joby's six-tilt-rotor DEP design — establishes the Joby six-rotor configuration prior art two years before the public S4 unveil (2018-12-20) and establishes the Joby Aviation patent estate priority chain.
sources: Bevirt, JoeBen et al. Joby S2 technical white paper, 2015.
Joby Aviation S-1 SEC filing 2020 (history section).
Vertical Flight Society Forum technical papers from Joby engineering 2015-2018.

creator: MACA Industries (Toulouse, France) / Michaël Carchidi
disclosure_citation: MACA Industries (founded 2019 in Toulouse, France) unveiled the S11 Carcopter — a hydrogen-fuel-cell racing eVTOL — at the September 2021 Munich IAA Mobility show. Designed for an envisioned hydrogen racing league. Documented in MACA materials and aerospace press.
ip_citations: MACA Industries patent estate, French / EU / PCT filings 2019-2024
prior_art_notes: MACA Carcopter establishes French prior art for the hydrogen-fuel-cell racing eVTOL — distinct from Alauda Airspeeder (AU, battery-electric racing) by the hydrogen powertrain. Together with airspeeder (AU) and amsl-vertiia (AU, hydrogen passenger), places hydrogen and racing eVTOL architecture in cross-national prior art. Adds another French entry to the deep French VTOL heritage (SNECMA Coléoptère, Eurocopter X3, Airbus RACER, Flying Whales, Cornu, Oehmichen, d'Amécourt, Lazareth, Airbus Vahana, Airbus Pop.Up, Verne).
propulsion_details: An eight-rotor racing multirotor (four pylons, each carrying a coaxial pair) with a Formula-1-inspired single-seat composite cockpit; hydrogen fuel cells with a small lithium-ion buffer battery; designed for high-speed pylon racing in an envisioned hydrogen-fuelled racing league. Distinct from Alauda Airspeeder (Australian battery-electric racing eVTOL) by the hydrogen powertrain.
sources: MACA Industries S11 Carcopter technical materials and IAA Mobility 2021 unveiling.
MACA Industries press materials 2021-2024.

creator: Bellwether Industries (London, United Kingdom)
disclosure_citation: Bellwether Industries (founded 2019 in London by Kai-Hsiang Lin) publicly disclosed the Volar — a four-seat ducted-fan flying-car concept designed to look like a luxury car — through 2021-2024 design and sub-scale-prototype releases. The 'Antelope' 1/6-scale demonstrator made tethered flights 2021. Documented in Bellwether materials.
ip_citations: Bellwether Industries Volar patent estate, UK / EU / PCT filings 2019-2024
prior_art_notes: Bellwether Industries' Volar concept (UK, 2019-) establishes prior art for the 'luxury-car-aesthetic flying car with internal ducted fans' — eight electric ducted lift fans buried inside a sleek closed-body vehicle with no exposed rotors, smooth aerodynamic surface in cruise. Distinct from Alef Model A (rotors under mesh skin, whole-body rotation) and from open-rotor multirotor eVTOLs. Adds UK depth to the flying-car / ducted-fan-array prior-art base alongside Lilium (DE), Aurora LightningStrike (US), Bell X-22 (US), Bell Nexus (US), Doak VZ-4 (US), Pegasus VBJ (ZA), Boeing Phantom Swift (US), and Horizon Aircraft Cavorite X5 (CA).
propulsion_details: Eight electric ducted lift fans buried inside a sleek closed-body vehicle (no exposed rotors) — the lift fans take air through louvered intakes and exit through louvered outlets, with the body surface staying smooth in cruise; intended hybrid-electric powertrain. The 'luxury-car-aesthetic flying car with internal ducted fans' design — distinct architecturally from the open-rotor multirotor and tilt-rotor eVTOL families. UK design lineage.
sources: Bellwether Industries Volar / Antelope technical materials 2019-2024.

creator: Ryan Coogler / Marvel Studios / Disney / Hannah Beachler (production design)
disclosure_citation: Black Panther, directed by Ryan Coogler, U.S. theatrical release 2018-02-16 — featuring the Royal Talon Fighter, the Wakandan flagship VTOL aircraft used by T'Challa and the Dora Milaje. Production design by Hannah Beachler (the first African American production designer to win an Academy Award, for this film). Documented in Marvel Studios Black Panther production design books and Beachler's design materials.
ip_citations: Marvel Studios / Disney Black Panther copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: The Royal Talon Fighter (Black Panther, Hannah Beachler production design, 2018) establishes a culturally-distinct US-cinema VTOL anchor — Wakandan/Afro-futurist visual-design aesthetics applied to a fictional combat VTOL. Adds to the global fictional VTOL prior-art base alongside the existing American (Aliens, Avatar, Iron Man, Bubbleship, BTTF, Fifth Element, Star Trek, Star Wars), Japanese (Macross, Patlabor, Ghost in the Shell, Gundam, Laputa, Nausicaä, Doraemon, Last Exile, Appleseed), British (Thunderbird 2), Soviet/Georgian (Kin-dza-dza), Argentinian (Eternauta), South African (District 9), Chinese (Wandering Earth), Indian (Krrish), and Korean (Space Sweepers) fictional anchors.
propulsion_details: A VTOL combat / transport aircraft with a Wakandan/Afro-futurist aesthetic — vibranium-skinned, sound-dampened, capable of cloaking, vertical takeoff, transition to high-speed cruise, hover, and vertical landing. The depicted lift mechanism is fictional (vibranium-augmented thrust). Hannah Beachler's design notes specify Afro-futurist-influenced engineering aesthetics — a deliberate departure from the typical Western-military VTOL visual language.
sources: Coogler, Ryan (dir.). Black Panther. Marvel Studios / Disney, 2018.
Beachler, Hannah. Black Panther production design materials.
The Art of Marvel Studios' Black Panther. Marvel, 2018.

aliases: ラスト エグザイル Vanship
creator: Koichi Chigira / Range Murata (mecha design) / Gonzo (studio)
disclosure_citation: Last Exile (ラスト エグザイル Rasuto Eguzairu), directed by Koichi Chigira, first broadcast 2003-04-07 on TV Tokyo (Japan). The Vanship — a small two-seat steampunk flying machine controlled by foot-pedals and a sidestick — is the series' iconic personal aircraft, designed by Range Murata. Documented in Last Exile production design books (Gonzo, 2003-2011) and Range Murata art books.
ip_citations: Gonzo / Last Exile copyright on the depiction; Range Murata design copyright; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: Range Murata's Vanship design (Last Exile, 2003) is a canonical steampunk-aesthetic Japanese fictional personal-VTOL — small two-seat, foot-pedal-and-sidestick controlled, with Claudia anti-grav lift plus rear-exhaust propulsion. Murata's published design materials provide engineering-grade depictions. Adds another Japanese fictional anchor with a distinctive 1920s-30s racing-aircraft aesthetic, complementing the futuristic-mecha (Macross, Patlabor, Ghost in the Shell, Gundam) and the Miyazaki organic (Laputa, Nausicaä) Japanese fictional VTOL clusters.
propulsion_details: A small two-seat (pilot in front, navigator behind) atmospheric racing/courier aircraft using fictional 'Claudia' technology (an alternate-history anti-grav substance) for lift plus a rear thrust exhaust for propulsion; foot-pedal-and-sidestick control. The Vanship is the canonical Range Murata steampunk personal-VTOL design — extensively detailed in Murata's published design materials. Inspired by 1920s-1930s racing aircraft aesthetics.
sources: Chigira, Koichi (dir.). Last Exile (ラスト エグザイル). Gonzo, 2003.
Murata, Range. Last Exile design materials and art books, Gonzo / Wani Books / Asuka Shinsha, 2003-2011.

creator: Masamune Shirow / Seishinsha / Bandai Visual
disclosure_citation: Masamune Shirow. Appleseed (アップルシード) manga, first serialized 1985-02 in Seishinsha's Comic Gaia (later collected as four volumes 1985-1989). Subsequent anime adaptations (1988 OVA, 2004 film, 2007 Appleseed Ex Machina, 2014 Appleseed Alpha) develop the depictions of the Landmate powered exoskeleton (with VTOL-augmented jumps in later versions) and the various Olympus VTOL military transports. Documented in Shirow's published manga, the Appleseed Databook (Seishinsha, 1990), and successor anime production materials.
ip_citations: Shirow / Seishinsha / Bandai Visual Appleseed copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: Masamune Shirow's Appleseed (manga 1985, anime adaptations through 2014) is one of the foundational Japanese cyberpunk-mecha franchises — featuring the Landmate powered exoskeleton (with VTOL-augmented leaps in later depictions) and detailed VTOL military transports. Adds another Shirow-designed entry to the corpus (with the existing ghost-in-the-shell-tiltrotor 1989) — Shirow is among the most engineering-rigorous mecha designers in anime history. Together with iron-man-suit (1963) and SoloTrek XFV (2001 real), places powered-exoskeleton VTOL architecture in cross-decade fictional + real prior art.
propulsion_details: The Landmate is a powered exoskeleton (the wearer drives like a vehicle from inside; the exo's arms are remote-actuated from joysticks) with VTOL-augmented jumps in later depictions; Appleseed's Olympus city also features various VTOL military transports — large tilt-rotor / vectored-thrust craft used by ESWAT (Special Weapons and Tactics). Shirow's mecha designs are engineering-detailed in his manga and the Appleseed Databook.
sources: Shirow, Masamune. Appleseed (アップルシード) manga, Seishinsha Comic Gaia, 1985-1989 (4 volumes).
Appleseed Databook. Seishinsha, 1990.
Appleseed anime adaptations: 1988 OVA, 2004 film (Shinji Aramaki dir.), 2007 Appleseed Ex Machina, 2014 Appleseed Alpha.

creator: Andrew Stanton / Pixar Animation Studios / Walt Disney Pictures
disclosure_citation: WALL-E, directed by Andrew Stanton, U.S. theatrical release 2008-06-27 — featuring the Axiom starship's anti-gravity hover-chairs, used by the entire population of Earth-evacuee passengers. Production design by Ralph Eggleston. Documented in The Art of WALL-E (Chronicle Books, 2008) and Pixar production materials.
ip_citations: Pixar / Disney WALL-E copyright on the depiction; engineering description unencumbered as prior art
prior_art_notes: WALL-E (Pixar/Disney, 2008) establishes the canonical cinematic depiction of mass-deployed personal hover-chairs — single-occupant anti-grav chairs replacing walking, operated by touchscreen, used ubiquitously. Adds another fictional_repulsor entry alongside star-wars-repulsorlift (1977), bttf-2-hoverboard (1989), hg-wells-cavorite (1901), blish-cities-in-flight (1955), arabian-nights-flying-carpet (~9th c), pushpaka-vimana (~5th c BCE). Establishes a specific fictional anchor for: (1) personal-scale anti-grav mobility devices, (2) seated/sitting-position anti-grav posture (distinct from the standing-platform hoverboard). Anticipates any patent claim around anti-grav personal mobility seating.
propulsion_details: Single-occupant chair with a built-in anti-gravity 'repulsor' base, operated by a touchscreen display in front of the occupant; the chair hovers a meter or so off the floor and can be commanded to move horizontally at walking-to-jogging pace. Mass-deployed personal-mobility hover devices, used by the entire 700-year-evacuee population of the Axiom starship. The canonical cinematic depiction of personal hover-chair anti-grav mobility.
sources: Stanton, Andrew (dir.). WALL-E. Pixar Animation Studios / Walt Disney Pictures, 2008.
Hauser, Tim. The Art of WALL-E. Chronicle Books, 2008.

aliases: Autonomous Rotorcraft Testbed for Intelligent Systems
control_architecture: autonomous mission management; onboard sense-and-avoid; supervised by ground operator
creator: Deutsches Zentrum für Luft- und Raumfahrt (DLR) Institute of Flight Systems / Stephan Adolf / Florian-Michael Adolf
disclosure_citation: DLR ARTIS (Autonomous Rotorcraft Testbed for Intelligent Systems) first flight 2005; SuperARTIS (larger 24 kg variant) entered service ~2010 at DLR Braunschweig. The German Aerospace Center's autonomous-rotorcraft research platform. Foundational reference paper: Adolf, F.M., Andert, F. 'Onboard mission management for a VTOL UAV using sequence and supervisory control.' AHS Forum, 2011. Subsequent papers through 2015-2020 expanded ARTIS / SuperARTIS capability.
ip_citations: DLR academic publications; open under DLR conventions
prior_art_notes: DLR ARTIS / SuperARTIS (2005-) is the German Aerospace Center's autonomous-rotorcraft research platform — adds DLR (the German national aerospace research agency) to the academic aerial-robotics prior-art base alongside the existing US (UPenn GRASP, MIT, Harvard, NASA Langley/Ames/Armstrong), Swiss (ETH, EPFL, UZH), Japanese (Tokyo JSK), Dutch (TU Delft), Italian (Bologna), Austrian (IAT-21, CycloTech), Australian (ArduPilot), and Swiss-academic (Crazyflie at Bitcraze) anchors. The DLR ARTIS publications (F.M. Adolf, F. Andert, et al.) document mission-management, sense-and-avoid, and autonomous-rotorcraft control through 2005-2020.
propulsion_details: A modified small commercial helicopter (Yamaha R-50 in ARTIS, larger platform in SuperARTIS) instrumented with onboard autonomy sensors (GPS/INS, vision, lidar) and computational hardware (Pentium-class embedded compute); flight-software stack for autonomous mission planning, obstacle avoidance, and rotorcraft maneuvering. The DLR-specific German autonomous-rotorcraft research platform — alongside the US-academic and Japanese-academic platforms (GRASP, ETH, MIT ACL, Tokyo JSK) that comprise the global academic aerial-robotics infrastructure.
sources: Adolf, F.M., Andert, F. 'Onboard mission management for a VTOL UAV using sequence and supervisory control.' AHS Forum, 2011.
DLR Institute of Flight Systems publications archive (Braunschweig).
Andert, F., Adolf, F.M. et al. ARTIS / SuperARTIS technical reports 2005-2020.

control_architecture: variable-pitch quadrotor with collective-pitch lift control; aerobatic flight envelopes; motion-capture localization
creator: MIT Aerospace Controls Laboratory (Jonathan How) / RAVEN testbed
disclosure_citation: MIT Aerospace Controls Laboratory (Jonathan How) developed the RAVEN (Real-time indoor Autonomous Vehicle test ENvironment) testbed and a long series of aggressive-quadrotor-maneuver publications from 2009 onward. Foundational paper: Cutler, M. and How, J.P. 'Analysis and Control of a Variable-Pitch Quadrotor for Agile Flight.' ASME Journal of Dynamic Systems, Measurement, and Control 137(10), 2015. Subsequent papers through 2020 cover aerobatic maneuvers, variable-pitch propellers, and learning-based control.
ip_citations: MIT academic publication; open under academic conventions
prior_art_notes: MIT Aerospace Controls Lab (Jonathan How) RAVEN-platform aggressive-quadrotor maneuver work (2009-2020) establishes US academic prior art for variable-pitch quadrotor architecture and aerobatic-envelope control. Distinct from fixed-pitch BLDC commodity multirotor designs by adding per-rotor collective-pitch control — relevant prior art for any eVTOL claim involving variable-pitch lift rotors (Joby S4, Archer Midnight, Vahana, and other modern eVTOL all use variable-pitch on at least some rotors). Adds MIT to the US academic aerial-robotics anchor set alongside UPenn GRASP, Harvard Microrobotics, and the various NASA / DARPA programs.
propulsion_details: Variable-pitch quadrotor architecture (the rotors have collective pitch control rather than just RPM control) enabling aerobatic maneuvers including inverted flight and continuous rolls; demonstrated on a custom indoor quadrotor in the RAVEN motion-capture facility. MIT ACL's variable-pitch-quadrotor disclosures are foundational for any eVTOL or multirotor claim involving collective-pitch lift rotors (distinct from fixed-pitch BLDC commodity multirotor design).
sources: Cutler, M., How, J.P. 'Analysis and Control of a Variable-Pitch Quadrotor for Agile Flight.' ASME JDSMC 137(10), 2015.
MIT Aerospace Controls Lab publications archive (RAVEN testbed).
How, J.P. et al. 'RAVEN: Indoor multi-vehicle UAV test environment.' AIAA GNC 2008.

aliases: Center for Autonomous Systems and Technologies
control_architecture: deep-learning-augmented adaptive control (Neural-Fly); multi-modal walking/flying robotic control (LEONARDO)
creator: Caltech Center for Autonomous Systems and Technologies / Soon-Jo Chung / Aaron Ames
disclosure_citation: Caltech CAST (Center for Autonomous Systems and Technologies) established 2016 at Caltech. Key publications: O'Connell, M. et al. 'Neural-Fly enables rapid learning for agile flight in strong winds.' Science Robotics 7(66), 2022 (a paper demonstrating deep-learning-augmented quadrotor control in high-wind environments); plus a long series of multi-modal flying-robot papers from Soon-Jo Chung's group (LEONARDO, the bipedal-robot-with-multirotor hybrid, 2021).
ip_citations: Caltech academic publication; open under academic conventions
prior_art_notes: Caltech CAST (Center for Autonomous Systems and Technologies, Soon-Jo Chung / Aaron Ames groups, 2016-) establishes US academic prior art for deep-learning-augmented quadrotor control (Neural-Fly, Science Robotics 2022) and for multi-modal walking/flying hybrid robots (LEONARDO, 2021). Relevant prior art for any eVTOL claim involving ML-augmented adaptive control or hybrid bipedal-flying robotic platforms. Adds Caltech to the US academic aerial-robotics anchor set alongside UPenn GRASP, MIT ACL, Harvard, Stanford, and the various NASA / DARPA programs.
propulsion_details: CAST's research platform includes a CAST drone arena (motion-capture indoor testbed) for testing quadrotor / multirotor / multi-modal flying robots, plus LEONARDO — a bipedal humanoid robot with body-mounted propellers ('multi-modal LEgs ONboARD drOne') that walks, balances, and flies (2021). The Neural-Fly paper (Science Robotics 2022) shows deep-learning meta-learning for high-wind quadrotor control. CAST is the modern US academic anchor for ML-augmented aerial-robotics control.
sources: O'Connell, M., Shi, G., Shi, X., Azizzadenesheli, K., Anandkumar, A., Yue, Y., Chung, S.-J. 'Neural-Fly enables rapid learning for agile flight in strong winds.' Science Robotics 7(66), 2022.
Kim, K., Spieler, P., Lupu, E.-S., Ramezani, A., Chung, S.-J. 'A bipedal walking robot that can fly, slackline, and skateboard (LEONARDO).' Science Robotics 6(59), 2021.
Caltech CAST publications archive.

control_architecture: remote-piloted or autonomous (later variants); single-main-rotor + tail-rotor stability
creator: Yamaha Motor Co.
disclosure_citation: Yamaha Motor Co. developed the R-50 unmanned helicopter (1987, the world's first commercial unmanned helicopter) and the production RMAX from 1990 — an autonomous helicopter primarily used for agricultural spraying. Used in Japan for crop dusting from 1990, now operating ~30% of all Japanese rice-paddy spraying. Foundational real-world deployment of autonomous rotorcraft. Documented in Yamaha Motor materials and Japanese MAFF (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries) records.
ip_citations: Yamaha Motor RMAX patent estate, Japanese / US filings 1985-2015
prior_art_notes: Yamaha Motor's RMAX (1990 production; R-50 predecessor 1987) is the world's first commercially-deployed unmanned rotorcraft and the foundational autonomous-rotorcraft for agricultural use — predating commercial multi-rotor drones by decades. Establishes Japanese industrial prior art for: (1) autonomous unmanned rotorcraft commercial operations, (2) agricultural-spray VTOL UAS, (3) the 30+-year deployment history of autonomous rotorcraft at scale. Yamaha is also the supplier of the powerplants for many other small aerial robotics platforms. Together with schiebel-camcopter-s-100 (AT, naval VTOL UAV), saab-skeldar-v-200 (SE, naval VTOL UAV), and pyka-pelican (US, agricultural eVTOL), places autonomous-rotorcraft commercial deployment in continuous prior art.
propulsion_details: A small single-main-rotor + tail-rotor unmanned helicopter (3.6 m main rotor diameter); two-cylinder two-stroke engine. Pilot uses a remote-control console at ground level; later RMAX Type II G adds GPS-based autonomous flight modes. The world's first commercially-deployed unmanned rotorcraft and the foundational autonomous-rotorcraft for agricultural use — predating commercial multi-rotor drones by decades.
sources: Yamaha Motor Co. RMAX technical materials 1990-2020.
Japanese MAFF (Ministry of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries) RMAX agricultural-spray records.
Sato, A. 'Civil UAV applications in Japan and related safety issues.' AIAA, 2003.

creator: Sikorsky Aircraft (Stratford, Connecticut)
disclosure_citation: Sikorsky Cypher first flight April 1995; demonstrated to U.S. Army, Air Force, and Marine Corps 1995-2002; Cypher II (with stub-wings for fixed-wing cruise mode) followed late 1990s. The first practical ducted-fan VTOL UAV from a major rotorcraft manufacturer. Documented in Sikorsky technical papers and U.S. military evaluation records.
ip_citations: Sikorsky Cypher patent estate, US filings 1990-2005
prior_art_notes: Sikorsky Cypher (1995) is the foundational ducted-fan VTOL UAV from a major rotorcraft manufacturer — a donut-shaped airframe with coaxial counter-rotating ducted lift rotors. Cypher II (late 1990s) added stub-wings for fixed-wing cruise mode, anticipating the lift+cruise architectural pattern that modern eVTOL adopt. Establishes prior art for: (1) ducted-fan VTOL UAV with coaxial lift rotors, (2) the donut/disc airframe with internal lift rotors, (3) the stub-wing-augmented ducted-fan UAV (Cypher II). Together with the various tail-sitter VTOL UAVs (aerovironment-skytote, volansi-vbat) and modern cargo VTOL UAVs (wingcopter, pyka, etc), places military/commercial VTOL UAV architecture in continuous prior art from 1995.
